declarations.type_signature: (ms: number, decimals?: number | undefined, separator?: string | undefined): string

declarations.name: print_number_with_separators
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a `number` with separators for improved readability.

declarations.name: print_string
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a `string` for printing.

declarations.name: print_number
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a `number` for printing.

declarations.name: print_boolean
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a `boolean` for printing.

declarations.name: print_value
declarations.doc_comment: Formats any value for printing.

declarations.name: print_error
declarations.doc_comment:
Formats an error for printing.
Because throwing errors and rejecting promises isn't typesafe,
don't assume the arg is an `Error` and try to return something useful.

declarations.name: print_timing
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a timing entry with `key` for printing.
declarations.type_signature: (key: string | number, timing: number | undefined): string

declarations.name: print_timings
declarations.doc_comment: Prints all timings in a `Timings` object.

declarations.name: SpawnResultError
declarations.doc_comment:
Spawn failed before the process could run.

Note: `child` is still present because `node:child_process.spawn` returns a
`ChildProcess` synchronously and then emits `'error'` asynchronously — the
handle exists but the OS process never started.
examples: ENOENT when command not found

declarations.name: SpawnResultExited
declarations.doc_comment:
Process ran and exited with a code.
`ok` is true when `code` is 0.

declarations.name: SpawnResultSignaled
declarations.doc_comment: Process was terminated by a signal (e.g., SIGTERM, SIGKILL).

declarations.name: SpawnResult
declarations.doc_comment:
Discriminated union representing all possible spawn outcomes.
Narrow via `result.kind === 'error' | 'exited' | 'signaled'`.

declarations.name: SpawnProcessOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Options for spawning processes, extending Node's `SpawnOptions`.

properties.name: signal
properties.doc_comment:
AbortSignal to cancel the process.
When aborted, sends SIGTERM to the child.

properties.name: timeout_ms
properties.doc_comment:
Timeout in milliseconds. Must be non-negative.
Sends SIGTERM when exceeded. A value of 0 triggers immediate SIGTERM.

properties.name: spawn_child_process
properties.doc_comment: Custom spawn function for testing. Defaults to `node:child_process` spawn.

declarations.name: DespawnOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Options for killing processes.

properties.name: signal
properties.doc_comment: Signal to send.

properties.name: timeout_ms
properties.doc_comment:
Timeout in ms before escalating to SIGKILL. Must be non-negative.
Useful for processes that may ignore SIGTERM. A value of 0 triggers immediate SIGKILL escalation.

declarations.name: SpawnDetachedResult
declarations.doc_comment: Result of spawning a detached process.

declarations.name: SpawnedProcess
declarations.doc_comment: Handle for a spawned process with access to the child and completion promise.

properties.name: child
properties.doc_comment: The underlying Node.js ChildProcess

properties.name: closed
properties.doc_comment: Resolves when the process exits

declarations.name: SpawnedOut
declarations.doc_comment: Result of `spawn_out` with captured output streams.

properties.name: stdout
properties.doc_comment: Captured stdout, or null if stream unavailable

properties.name: stderr
properties.doc_comment: Captured stderr, or null if stream unavailable

declarations.name: ProcessRegistry
declarations.doc_comment:
Manages a collection of spawned processes for lifecycle tracking and cleanup.

The default instance `process_registry_default` is used by module-level functions.
Create separate instances for isolated process groups or testing.

members.name: processes
members.doc_comment: All currently tracked child processes

members.name: spawn
members.doc_comment:
Spawns a process and tracks it in this registry.
The process is automatically unregistered when it exits.
members.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): SpawnedProcess

throws.description: if `timeout_ms` is negative

members.name: spawn_out
members.doc_comment:
Spawns a process and captures stdout/stderr as strings.
Sets `stdio: 'pipe'` automatically.
members.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): Promise<SpawnedOut>
members.return_description:
result with captured `stdout` and `stderr`
- `null` means spawn failed (ENOENT, etc.) or stream was unavailable
- `''` (empty string) means process ran but produced no output
- non-empty string contains the captured output

throws.description: if `timeout_ms` is negative

members.name: despawn
members.doc_comment: Kills a child process and waits for it to exit.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: kill options including signal and timeout

throws.description: if `timeout_ms` is negative

members.name: despawn_all
members.doc_comment: Kills all processes in this registry.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: kill options applied to all processes

members.name: attach_error_handler
members.doc_comment:
Attaches an `uncaughtException` handler that kills all processes before exiting.
Prevents zombie processes when the parent crashes.

By default uses SIGKILL for immediate termination. Set `graceful_timeout_ms`
to attempt SIGTERM first (allowing processes to clean up) before escalating
to SIGKILL after the timeout.

Note: Node's uncaughtException handler cannot await async operations, so
graceful shutdown uses a blocking busy-wait. This may not be sufficient
for processes that need significant cleanup time.
members.type_signature: (options?: { to_error_label?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; map_error_text?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; handle_error?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => void) | undefined; graceful_timeout_ms?: number | ... 1 more ... | undefined; } | undefined): () => void
members.return_description: cleanup function to remove the handler

parameters.name: options
parameters.type: { to_error_label?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; map_error_text?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; handle_error?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => void) | undefined; graceful_timeout_ms?: number | ... 1 more...

throws.description: if a handler is already attached to this registry

declarations.name: process_registry_default
declarations.doc_comment:
Default process registry used by module-level spawn functions.
For testing or isolated process groups, create a new `ProcessRegistry` instance.

declarations.name: spawn_process
declarations.doc_comment:
Spawns a process with graceful shutdown behavior.
Returns a handle with access to the `child` process and `closed` promise.
declarations.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): SpawnedProcess

declarations.name: spawn
declarations.doc_comment:
Spawns a process and returns a promise that resolves when it exits.
Use this for commands that complete (not long-running processes).
declarations.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): Promise<SpawnResult>

declarations.name: spawn_out
declarations.doc_comment: Spawns a process and captures stdout/stderr as strings.
declarations.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): Promise<SpawnedOut>

declarations.name: despawn
declarations.doc_comment: Kills a child process and returns the result.

declarations.name: despawn_all
declarations.doc_comment: Kills all processes in the default registry.

declarations.name: attach_process_error_handler
declarations.doc_comment: Attaches an `uncaughtException` handler to the default registry.
declarations.type_signature: (options?: { to_error_label?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; map_error_text?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; handle_error?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => void) | undefined; graceful_timeout_ms?: number | ... 1 more ... | undefined; } | undefined): () => void

parameters.name: options
parameters.type: { to_error_label?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; map_error_text?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => string | null) | undefined; handle_error?: ((err: Error, origin: UncaughtExceptionOrigin) => void) | undefined; graceful_timeout_ms?: number | ... 1 more...

declarations.name: spawn_detached
declarations.doc_comment:
Spawns a detached process that continues after parent exits.

Unlike other spawn functions, this is NOT tracked in any `ProcessRegistry`.
The spawned process is meant to outlive the parent (e.g., daemon processes).
declarations.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnOptions | undefined): SpawnDetachedResult
declarations.return_description: result with pid on success, or error message on failure

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: spawn options (use `stdio` to redirect output to file descriptors)

declarations.name: print_child_process
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a child process for display.
examples:
```ts
`pid(1234) <- node server.js`
```

declarations.name: print_spawn_result
declarations.doc_comment:
Formats a spawn result for display.
Returns `'ok'` for success, or the error/signal/code for failures.

declarations.name: spawn_result_to_message
declarations.doc_comment: Formats a spawn result for use in error messages.

declarations.name: RestartableProcess
declarations.doc_comment:
Handle for a process that can be restarted.
Exposes `closed` promise for observing exits and implementing restart policies.

properties.name: restart
properties.doc_comment:
Restart the process, killing the current one if active.
Concurrent calls are coalesced - multiple calls before the first completes
will share the same restart operation.

properties.name: kill
properties.doc_comment: Kill the process and set `active` to false

properties.name: active
properties.doc_comment:
Whether this handle is managing a process.

Note: This reflects handle state, not whether the underlying OS process is executing.
Remains `true` after a process exits naturally until `kill()` or `restart()` is called.
To check if the process actually exited, await `closed`.

properties.name: child
properties.doc_comment: The current child process, or null if not active

properties.name: closed
properties.doc_comment: Promise that resolves when the current process exits

properties.name: spawned
properties.doc_comment:
Promise that resolves when the initial `spawn_process()` call completes.

Note: This resolves when the spawn syscall returns, NOT when the process
is "ready" or has produced output. For commands that fail immediately
(e.g., ENOENT), `spawned` still resolves - check `closed` for errors.

declarations.name: spawn_restartable_process
declarations.doc_comment:
Spawns a process that can be restarted.
Handles concurrent restart calls gracefully.

Note: The `signal` and `timeout_ms` options are reapplied on each restart.
If the AbortSignal is already aborted when `restart()` is called, the new
process will be killed immediately.
declarations.type_signature: (command: string, args?: readonly string[], options?: SpawnProcessOptions | undefined): RestartableProcess

declarations.name: process_is_pid_running
declarations.doc_comment:
Checks if a process with the given PID is running.
Uses signal 0 which checks existence without sending a signal.
declarations.return_description:
`true` if the process exists (even without permission to signal it),
`false` if the process doesn't exist or if pid is invalid (non-positive, non-integer, NaN, Infinity)

parameters.name: pid
parameters.description: the process ID to check (must be a positive integer)

declarations.name: RandomAlea
declarations.doc_comment:
Alea: a seedable pseudo-random number generator by Johannes Baagøe.
Supports variadic and string seeds (`create_random_alea('my', 3, 'seeds')`).
For numeric seeds, prefer `create_random_xoshiro` which is faster with equal quality.

DO NOT USE when security matters — use the Web Crypto API (`crypto.getRandomValues`) instead.

Alea passes all 11 distribution quality tests at 10M samples,
performing on par with `Math.random` (V8's xorshift128+):

- mean, variance, chi-squared uniformity, Kolmogorov-Smirnov
- lag-1 through lag-8 autocorrelation
- runs test, gap test, permutation test (triples)
- bit-level frequency (bits 0-7)
- 2D serial pairs (25x25 through 200x200 grids)
- birthday spacings (Marsaglia parameters)

Speed is ~19% slower than `Math.random` (~12.2M ops/sec vs ~15.0M ops/sec).

To reproduce:

```bash
npm run benchmark_random_quality          # distribution tests (N=1M)
npm run benchmark_random_quality -- --deep # thorough (N=10M, multi-trial)
npm run benchmark_random                  # speed comparison
```

declarations.name: create_random_alea
declarations.doc_comment:
Seeded pseudo-random number generator.
DO NOT USE when security matters, use webcrypto APIs instead.

modules.module_comment:
Alea: a seedable pseudo-random number generator by Johannes Baagøe.
Supports variadic and string seeds (`create_random_alea('my', 3, 'seeds')`).
For numeric seeds, prefer `create_random_xoshiro` which is faster with equal quality.

DO NOT USE when security matters — use the Web Crypto API (`crypto.getRandomValues`) instead.

Alea passes all 11 distribution quality tests at 10M samples,
performing on par with `Math.random` (V8's xorshift128+):

- mean, variance, chi-squared uniformity, Kolmogorov-Smirnov
- lag-1 through lag-8 autocorrelation
- runs test, gap test, permutation test (triples)
- bit-level frequency (bits 0-7)
- 2D serial pairs (25x25 through 200x200 grids)
- birthday spacings (Marsaglia parameters)

Speed is ~19% slower than `Math.random` (~12.2M ops/sec vs ~15.0M ops/sec).

To reproduce:

```bash
npm run benchmark_random_quality          # distribution tests (N=1M)
npm run benchmark_random_quality -- --deep # thorough (N=10M, multi-trial)
npm run benchmark_random                  # speed comparison
```

@see https://github.com/nquinlan/better-random-numbers-for-javascript-mirror

declarations.name: RandomXoshiro
declarations.doc_comment:
Xoshiro128**: a seedable pseudo-random number generator by Blackman & Vigna (2018).
Recommended for reproducible randomness (testing, simulations, procedural generation).

DO NOT USE when security matters — use the Web Crypto API (`crypto.getRandomValues`) instead.

Xoshiro128** has a 2^128-1 period and passes BigCrush.
It uses pure 32-bit arithmetic (shifts, rotates, XOR, multiply)
making it very fast in JavaScript via `Math.imul`.

Xoshiro128** passes all 11 distribution quality tests at 10M samples,
performing on par with `Math.random` (V8's xorshift128+):

- mean, variance, chi-squared uniformity, Kolmogorov-Smirnov
- lag-1 through lag-8 autocorrelation
- runs test, gap test, permutation test (triples)
- bit-level frequency (bits 0-7)
- 2D serial pairs (25x25 through 200x200 grids)
- birthday spacings (Marsaglia parameters)

Speed is near-native (~4% slower than `Math.random`) and ~18% faster than Alea
(~14.4M ops/sec vs ~15.0M and ~12.2M respectively).

To reproduce:

```bash
npm run benchmark_random_quality          # distribution tests (N=1M)
npm run benchmark_random_quality -- --deep # thorough (N=10M, multi-trial)
npm run benchmark_random                  # speed comparison
```

declarations.name: create_random_xoshiro
declarations.doc_comment:
Seeded pseudo-random number generator using the Xoshiro128** algorithm.
DO NOT USE when security matters, use webcrypto APIs instead.

modules.module_comment:
Xoshiro128**: a seedable pseudo-random number generator by Blackman & Vigna (2018).
Recommended for reproducible randomness (testing, simulations, procedural generation).

DO NOT USE when security matters — use the Web Crypto API (`crypto.getRandomValues`) instead.

Xoshiro128** has a 2^128-1 period and passes BigCrush.
It uses pure 32-bit arithmetic (shifts, rotates, XOR, multiply)
making it very fast in JavaScript via `Math.imul`.

Xoshiro128** passes all 11 distribution quality tests at 10M samples,
performing on par with `Math.random` (V8's xorshift128+):

- mean, variance, chi-squared uniformity, Kolmogorov-Smirnov
- lag-1 through lag-8 autocorrelation
- runs test, gap test, permutation test (triples)
- bit-level frequency (bits 0-7)
- 2D serial pairs (25x25 through 200x200 grids)
- birthday spacings (Marsaglia parameters)

Speed is near-native (~4% slower than `Math.random`) and ~18% faster than Alea
(~14.4M ops/sec vs ~15.0M and ~12.2M respectively).

To reproduce:

```bash
npm run benchmark_random_quality          # distribution tests (N=1M)
npm run benchmark_random_quality -- --deep # thorough (N=10M, multi-trial)
npm run benchmark_random                  # speed comparison
```

@see https://prng.di.unimi.it/
@see https://vigna.di.unimi.it/ftp/papers/ScrambledLinear.pdf

declarations.name: random_float
declarations.doc_comment: Generates a random `number` between `min` and `max`.
declarations.type_signature: (min: number, max: number, random?: () => number): number

declarations.name: random_int
declarations.doc_comment:
Returns a random integer between `min` and `max` inclusive.
Node's `randomInt` is similar but exclusive of the max value, and makes `min` optional -
https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v20.x/api/crypto.html#cryptorandomintmin-max-callback
declarations.type_signature: (min: number, max: number, random?: () => number): number

declarations.name: random_boolean
declarations.doc_comment: Generates a random `boolean`.

declarations.name: random_item
declarations.doc_comment: Selects a random item from an array.
declarations.type_signature: <T extends ReadonlyArray<any>>(arr: T, random?: () => number): ArrayElement<T>

declarations.name: shuffle
declarations.doc_comment: Mutates `array` with random ordering.
declarations.type_signature: <T extends Array<any>>(array: T, random?: ((min: number, max: number, random?: () => number) => number) | undefined): T

parameters.name: random
parameters.type: ((min: number, max: number, random?: () => number) => number) | undefined

modules.module_comment:
Random helpers that accept an optional `random` parameter,
defaulting to `Math.random`. Pass a seeded PRNG for reproducible results:

- `create_random_xoshiro` — fast, high-quality numeric seeding (recommended)
- `create_random_alea` — supports string and variadic seeds

declarations.name: escape_regexp
declarations.doc_comment: Escapes a string for literal matching in a RegExp.

declarations.name: reset_regexp
declarations.doc_comment:
Reset a RegExp's lastIndex to 0 for global and sticky patterns.
Ensures consistent behavior by clearing state that affects subsequent matches.

declarations.name: Result
declarations.doc_comment:
An alternative pattern to throwing and catching errors.
Uses the type system to strongly type error return values when desired.
Catching errors is then reserved for unexpected situations.

declarations.name: OK
declarations.doc_comment: Frozen object representing a successful result.

declarations.name: NOT_OK
declarations.doc_comment: Frozen object representing a failed result.

declarations.name: unwrap
declarations.doc_comment:
A helper that says,
"hey I know this is wrapped in a `Result`, but I expect it to be `ok`,
so if it's not, I understand it will throw an error that wraps the result".

throws.description: if `result.ok` is false

declarations.type_signature: <TValue extends { value?: unknown; }, TError extends { message?: string; }>(result: Result<TValue, TError>, message?: string | undefined): TValue["value"]

declarations.name: ResultError
declarations.doc_comment:
A custom error class that's thrown by `unwrap`.
Wraps a failed `Result` with an optional message,
and also accepts an optional message that overrides the result's.
Useful for generic handling of unwrapped results
to forward custom messages and other failed result data.

members.name: constructor
members.type_signature: (result: { ok: false; message?: string | undefined; }, message?: string | undefined, options?: ErrorOptions | undefined): ResultError

declarations.name: unwrap_error
declarations.doc_comment:
A helper that does the opposite of `unwrap`, throwing if the `Result` is ok.
Note that while `unwrap` returns the wrapped `value`, `unwrap_error` returns the entire result.

throws.description: if `result.ok` is true

declarations.type_signature: <TError extends object>(result: Result<object, TError>, message?: string): { ok: false; } & TError

declarations.name: Sortable
declarations.doc_comment: Minimum shape required for topological sorting.

declarations.name: TopologicalSortResult
declarations.doc_comment: Result of topological sort.

declarations.name: topological_sort
declarations.doc_comment:
Sort items by their dependencies using Kahn's algorithm.

Returns items ordered so that dependencies come before dependents.
If a cycle is detected, returns an error with the cycle path.
declarations.type_signature: <T extends Sortable>(items: T[], label?: string): TopologicalSortResult<T>
declarations.return_description: sorted items or error if cycle detected

modules.module_comment:
Generic topological sort using Kahn's algorithm.

Orders items so that dependencies come before dependents.
Works with any item type that has `id` and optional `depends_on`.

declarations.name: DeclarationKind
declarations.doc_comment: The kind of exported declaration.
declarations.type_signature: ZodEnum<{ function: "function"; type: "type"; variable: "variable"; class: "class"; constructor: "constructor"; component: "component"; json: "json"; css: "css"; }>

declarations.name: GenericParamInfo
declarations.doc_comment: Generic type parameter information.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; constraint: ZodOptional<ZodString>; default_type: ZodOptional<ZodString>; }, $loose>

declarations.name: ParameterInfo
declarations.doc_comment:
Parameter information for functions and methods.

Kept distinct from `ComponentPropInfo` despite structural similarity.
Function parameters form a tuple with positional semantics:
calling order matters (`fn(a, b)` vs `fn(b, a)`),
may include rest parameters and destructuring patterns.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; type: ZodString; optional: ZodOptional<ZodBoolean>; description: ZodOptional<ZodString>; default_value: ZodOptional<...>; }, $loose>

declarations.name: ComponentPropInfo
declarations.doc_comment:
Component prop information for Svelte components.

Kept distinct from `ParameterInfo` despite structural similarity.
Component props are named attributes with different semantics:
no positional order when passing (`<Foo {a} {b} />` = `<Foo {b} {a} />`),
support two-way binding via `$bindable` rune.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; type: ZodString; optional: ZodOptional<ZodBoolean>; description: ZodOptional<ZodString>; ... 6 more ...; since: ZodOptional<...>; }, $loose>

declarations.name: DeclarationJson
declarations.doc_comment:
Metadata for an exported declaration (function, type, class, component, etc.).

Extracted from TypeScript source and JSDoc/TSDoc comments at build time.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; kind: ZodEnum<{ function: "function"; type: "type"; variable: "variable"; class: "class"; constructor: "constructor"; component: "component"; json: "json"; css: "css"; }>; ... 20 more ...; alias_of: ZodOptional<...>; }, $loose>

declarations.name: ModuleJson
declarations.doc_comment: A source file in `src/lib/` with its exported declarations.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ path: ZodString; declarations: ZodOptional<ZodArray<ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; kind: ZodEnum<{ function: "function"; type: "type"; variable: "variable"; class: "class"; constructor: "constructor"; component: "component"; json: "json"; css: "css"; }>; ... 20 more ...; alias_of: ZodOptional<...>; }, $loo...

declarations.name: SourceJson
declarations.doc_comment: Metadata for a library's `src/lib/` exports.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; version: ZodString; modules: ZodOptional<ZodArray<ZodObject<{ path: ZodString; declarations: ZodOptional<ZodArray<ZodObject<{ name: ZodString; kind: ZodEnum<{ function: "function"; ... 6 more ...; css: "css"; }>; ... 20 more ...; alias_of: ZodOptional<...>; }, $loose>>>; module_comment: ...

declarations.name: declaration_get_display_name
declarations.doc_comment: Format declaration name with generic parameters for display.
examples:
```ts
declaration_get_display_name({name: 'Map', kind: 'type', generic_params: [{name: 'K'}, {name: 'V'}]})
// => 'Map<K, V>'
```
declarations.type_signature: (declaration: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; kind: "function" | "type" | "variable" | "class" | "constructor" | "component" | "json" | "css"; doc_comment?: string | undefined; type_signature?: string | undefined; ... 18 more ...; alias_of?: { ...; } | undefined; }): string

parameters.name: declaration
parameters.type: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; kind: "function" | "type" | "variable" | "class" | "constructor" | "component" | "json" | "css"; doc_comment?: string | undefined; type_signature?: string | undefined; ... 18 more ...; alias_of?: { ...; } | undefined; }

declarations.name: declaration_generate_import
declarations.doc_comment: Generate TypeScript import statement for a declaration.
examples:
```ts
declaration_generate_import({name: 'Foo', kind: 'type'}, 'foo.ts', '@pkg/lib')
// => "import type {Foo} from '@pkg/lib/foo.js';"
```
declarations.type_signature: (declaration: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; kind: "function" | "type" | "variable" | "class" | "constructor" | "component" | "json" | "css"; doc_comment?: string | undefined; type_signature?: string | undefined; ... 18 more ...; alias_of?: { ...; } | undefined; }, module_path: string, library_name: string): string

parameters.name: declaration
parameters.type: { [x: string]: unknown; name: string; kind: "function" | "type" | "variable" | "class" | "constructor" | "component" | "json" | "css"; doc_comment?: string | undefined; type_signature?: string | undefined; ... 18 more ...; alias_of?: { ...; } | undefined; }

modules.module_comment:
Metadata types for library source code analysis.

These types represent the structure of `src/lib/` exports,
extracted at build time via TypeScript compiler analysis.
Used for generating API documentation and enabling code search.

Hierarchy: `SourceJson` → `ModuleJson` → `DeclarationJson`

declarations.name: stats_mean
declarations.doc_comment: Calculate the mean (average) of an array of numbers.

declarations.name: stats_median
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate the median of an array of numbers.
NaN values are filtered out before computing.

declarations.name: stats_std_dev
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate the standard deviation of an array of numbers.
Uses population standard deviation (divides by n, not n-1).
For benchmarks with many samples, this is typically appropriate.

declarations.name: stats_variance
declarations.doc_comment: Calculate the variance of an array of numbers.

declarations.name: stats_percentile
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate a percentile of an array of numbers using linear interpolation.
Uses the "R-7" method (default in R, NumPy, Excel) which interpolates between
data points for more accurate percentile estimates, especially with smaller samples.

declarations.name: stats_cv
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate the coefficient of variation (CV).
CV = standard deviation / mean, expressed as a ratio.
Useful for comparing relative variability between datasets.

declarations.name: stats_min_max
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate min and max values.
NaN values are ignored.

declarations.name: StatsOutlierResult
declarations.doc_comment: Result from outlier detection.

properties.name: cleaned
properties.doc_comment: Values after removing outliers

properties.name: outliers
properties.doc_comment: Detected outlier values

declarations.name: StatsOutliersIqrOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Configuration options for IQR outlier detection.

properties.name: iqr_multiplier
properties.doc_comment: Multiplier for IQR bounds (default: 1.5)

properties.name: min_sample_size
properties.doc_comment: Minimum sample size to perform outlier detection (default: 3)

declarations.name: stats_outliers_iqr
declarations.doc_comment:
Detect outliers using the IQR (Interquartile Range) method.
Values outside [Q1 - multiplier*IQR, Q3 + multiplier*IQR] are considered outliers.

declarations.name: StatsOutliersMadOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Configuration options for MAD outlier detection.

properties.name: z_score_threshold
properties.doc_comment: Modified Z-score threshold for outlier detection (default: 3.5)

properties.name: z_score_extreme
properties.doc_comment: Extreme Z-score threshold when too many outliers detected (default: 5.0)

properties.name: mad_constant
properties.doc_comment: MAD constant for normal distribution (default: 0.6745)

properties.name: outlier_ratio_high
properties.doc_comment: Ratio threshold to switch to extreme mode (default: 0.3)

properties.name: outlier_ratio_extreme
properties.doc_comment: Ratio threshold to switch to keep-closest mode (default: 0.4)

properties.name: outlier_keep_ratio
properties.doc_comment: Ratio of values to keep in keep-closest mode (default: 0.8)

properties.name: min_sample_size
properties.doc_comment: Minimum sample size to perform outlier detection (default: 3)

properties.name: iqr_options
properties.doc_comment: Options to pass to IQR fallback when MAD is zero

declarations.name: stats_outliers_mad
declarations.doc_comment:
Detect outliers using the MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) method.
More robust than IQR for skewed distributions.
Uses modified Z-score: |0.6745 * (x - median) / MAD|
Values with modified Z-score > threshold are considered outliers.

declarations.name: STATS_CONFIDENCE_Z_SCORES
declarations.doc_comment: Common z-scores for confidence intervals.

declarations.name: stats_confidence_level_to_z_score
declarations.doc_comment:
Convert a confidence level (0-1) to a z-score.
Uses a lookup table for common values, approximates others.
examples:
```ts
stats_confidence_level_to_z_score(0.95); // 1.96
stats_confidence_level_to_z_score(0.99); // 2.576
```

throws.description: if `level` is not in the open interval (0, 1)

declarations.name: StatsConfidenceIntervalOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Configuration options for confidence interval calculation.

properties.name: z_score
properties.doc_comment: Z-score for confidence level (default: 1.96 for 95% CI)

properties.name: confidence_level
properties.doc_comment: Confidence level (0-1), alternative to z_score. If both provided, z_score takes precedence.

declarations.name: stats_confidence_interval
declarations.doc_comment: Calculate confidence interval for the mean.
declarations.return_description: `[lower_bound, upper_bound]`

declarations.name: stats_confidence_interval_from_summary
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate confidence interval from summary statistics (mean, std_dev, sample_size).
Useful when raw data is not available.
declarations.type_signature: (mean: number, std_dev: number, sample_size: number, options?: StatsConfidenceIntervalOptions | undefined): [number, number]
declarations.return_description: `[lower_bound, upper_bound]`

declarations.name: StatsWelchTTestResult
declarations.doc_comment: Result from Welch's t-test calculation.

properties.name: t_statistic
properties.doc_comment: The t-statistic

properties.name: degrees_of_freedom
properties.doc_comment: Welch-Satterthwaite degrees of freedom

declarations.name: stats_welch_t_test
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate Welch's t-test statistic and degrees of freedom.
Welch's t-test is more robust than Student's t-test when variances are unequal.

Params suffixed `1` describe the first sample, `2` the second.
declarations.type_signature: (mean1: number, std1: number, n1: number, mean2: number, std2: number, n2: number): StatsWelchTTestResult

declarations.name: stats_normal_cdf
declarations.doc_comment: Standard normal CDF approximation (Abramowitz and Stegun formula 7.1.26).

declarations.name: stats_ln_gamma
declarations.doc_comment: Log gamma function approximation (Lanczos approximation).

declarations.name: stats_incomplete_beta
declarations.doc_comment:
Approximate regularized incomplete beta function for p-value calculation.
Uses continued fraction expansion for reasonable accuracy.
declarations.type_signature: (x: number, a: number, b: number): number

declarations.name: stats_t_distribution_p_value
declarations.doc_comment:
Approximate two-tailed p-value from t-distribution.
For large df (>100), uses normal approximation.
For smaller df, uses incomplete beta function.
declarations.return_description: two-tailed p-value

modules.module_comment:
Statistical analysis utilities.
Pure functions with zero dependencies - can be used standalone for any data analysis.

declarations.name: truncate
declarations.doc_comment: Truncates a string to a maximum length, adding a suffix if needed that defaults to `...`.
declarations.type_signature: (str: string, maxLength: number, suffix?: string): string

declarations.name: strip_start
declarations.doc_comment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: strip_end
declarations.doc_comment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: strip_after
declarations.doc_comment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: strip_before
declarations.doc_comment: Removes characters inclusive of `stripped`.

declarations.name: ensure_start
declarations.doc_comment: Adds the substring `ensured` to the start of the `source` string if it's not already present.

declarations.name: ensure_end
declarations.doc_comment: Adds the substring `ensured` to the end of the `source` string if it's not already present.

declarations.name: deindent
declarations.doc_comment: Removes leading and trailing spaces from each line of a string.

declarations.name: plural
declarations.doc_comment: Returns a plural suffix based on a count.
declarations.type_signature: (count: number | null | undefined, suffix?: string): string

declarations.name: count_graphemes
declarations.doc_comment: Returns the count of graphemes in a string, the individually rendered characters.

declarations.name: strip_ansi
declarations.doc_comment: Strips ANSI escape sequences from a string.

declarations.name: stringify
declarations.doc_comment: Stringifies a value like `JSON.stringify` but with some corner cases handled better.

declarations.name: string_display_width
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculate the display width of a string in terminal columns.
- Strips ANSI escape codes (they have 0 width)
- Emojis and other wide characters take 2 columns
- Tab characters take 4 columns
- Newlines and other control characters take 0 columns
- Uses `Intl.Segmenter` to properly handle grapheme clusters (e.g., family emoji "👨‍👩‍👧‍👦")

declarations.name: pad_width
declarations.doc_comment: Pad a string to a target display width (accounting for wide characters).

declarations.name: levenshtein_distance
declarations.doc_comment:
Calculates the Levenshtein distance between two strings.
Useful for typo detection and fuzzy matching.
declarations.return_description: the edit distance between the strings

declarations.name: escape_js_string
declarations.doc_comment:
Escapes a string for use inside a single-quoted JS string literal.

Uses a single-pass regex replacement to escape backslashes, single quotes,
newlines, carriage returns, and Unicode line/paragraph separators.

declarations.name: string_is_binary
declarations.doc_comment:
Check if content appears to be binary.

Checks for null bytes in the first 8KB of content.

declarations.name: PreprocessImportInfo
declarations.doc_comment: Import metadata for a single import specifier.

properties.name: path
properties.doc_comment: The module path to import from.

properties.name: kind
properties.doc_comment: Whether this is a default or named import.

declarations.name: ResolvedComponentImport
declarations.doc_comment: Information about a resolved component import.

properties.name: import_node
properties.doc_comment: The `ImportDeclaration` AST node that provides this name.

properties.name: specifier
properties.doc_comment: The specific import specifier for this name.

declarations.name: find_attribute
declarations.doc_comment:
Finds an attribute by name on a component AST node.

Iterates the node's `attributes` array and returns the first `Attribute`
node whose `name` matches. Skips `SpreadAttribute`, directive, and other node types.
declarations.return_description: the matching `Attribute` node, or `undefined` if not found

declarations.name: evaluate_static_expr
declarations.doc_comment:
Recursively evaluates an expression AST node to a static string value.

Handles string `Literal`, `TemplateLiteral` (including interpolations when all
expressions resolve), `BinaryExpression` with `+`, and `Identifier` lookup
via an optional bindings map built by `build_static_bindings`.
Returns `null` for dynamic expressions, non-string literals, or unsupported node types.
declarations.type_signature: (expr: Expression, bindings?: ReadonlyMap<string, string> | undefined): string | null
declarations.return_description: the resolved static string, or `null` if the expression is dynamic

parameters.name: bindings
parameters.description: optional map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.name: extract_static_string
declarations.doc_comment:
Extracts a static string value from a Svelte attribute value AST node.

Handles three forms:
- Boolean `true` (bare attribute like `inline`) -- returns `null`.
- Array with a single `Text` node (quoted attribute like `content="text"`) --
  returns the text data.
- `ExpressionTag` (expression like `content={'text'}`) -- delegates to `evaluate_static_expr`.

Returns `null` for null literals, mixed arrays, dynamic expressions, and non-string values.
declarations.type_signature: (value: true | ExpressionTag | (ExpressionTag | Text)[], bindings?: ReadonlyMap<string, string> | undefined): string | null
declarations.return_description: the resolved static string, or `null` if the value is dynamic

parameters.name: bindings
parameters.description: optional map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.name: ConditionalChainBranch
declarations.doc_comment: A single branch in a conditional chain extracted from nested ternary expressions.

properties.name: test_source
properties.doc_comment: The source text of the test expression, or `null` for the final else branch.

properties.name: value
properties.doc_comment: The resolved static string value for this branch.

declarations.name: try_extract_conditional_chain
declarations.doc_comment:
Extracts a chain of conditional expressions where all leaf values are static strings.

Handles nested ternaries like `a ? 'x' : b ? 'y' : 'z'` by iteratively walking
the right-recursive `ConditionalExpression` chain. At each level, evaluates the
consequent via `evaluate_static_expr` and continues into the alternate if it is
another `ConditionalExpression`. The final alternate is the else branch.

Returns `null` if the attribute value is not an `ExpressionTag` containing a
`ConditionalExpression`, if any leaf fails to resolve to a static string, or
if the chain exceeds 10 branches (safety limit).

A 2-branch result covers the simple ternary case (`a ? 'x' : 'y'`).
declarations.type_signature: (value: true | ExpressionTag | (ExpressionTag | Text)[], source: string, bindings: ReadonlyMap<string, string>): ConditionalChainBranch[] | null
declarations.return_description: array of conditional chain branches, or `null` if not extractable

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: the full source string (needed to slice test expression source text)

parameters.name: bindings
parameters.description: map of variable names to their resolved static string values

declarations.name: build_static_bindings
declarations.doc_comment:
Builds a map of statically resolvable `const` bindings from a Svelte AST.

Scans top-level `const` variable declarations in both instance and module scripts.
For each declarator with a plain `Identifier` pattern and a statically evaluable
initializer, adds the binding to the map. Processes declarations in source order
so that chained references resolve: `const a = 'x'; const b = a;` maps `b` to `'x'`.

Skips destructuring patterns, `let`/`var` declarations, and declarations
whose initializers reference dynamic values.
declarations.return_description: map of variable names to their resolved static string values

parameters.name: ast
parameters.description: the parsed Svelte AST root node

declarations.name: resolve_component_names
declarations.doc_comment:
Resolves local names that import from specified source paths.

Scans `ImportDeclaration` nodes in both the instance and module scripts.
Handles default, named, and aliased imports. Skips namespace imports
and `import type` declarations (both whole-declaration and per-specifier).
Returns import node references alongside names to support import removal.
declarations.return_description: map of local names to their resolved import info

parameters.name: ast
parameters.description: the parsed Svelte AST root node

parameters.name: component_imports
parameters.description: array of import source paths to match against

declarations.name: find_import_insert_position
declarations.doc_comment:
Finds the position to insert new import statements within a script block.

Returns the end position of the last `ImportDeclaration`, or the start
of the script body content if no imports exist.

declarations.name: generate_import_lines
declarations.doc_comment:
Generates indented import statement lines from an import map.

Default imports produce `import Name from 'path';` lines.
Named imports are grouped by path into `import {a, b} from 'path';` lines.
declarations.return_description: a string of newline-separated import statements

parameters.name: imports
parameters.description: map of local names to their import info

declarations.name: has_identifier_in_tree
declarations.doc_comment:
Checks if an identifier with the given name appears anywhere in an AST subtree.

Recursively walks all object and array properties of the tree, matching
ESTree `Identifier` nodes (`{type: 'Identifier', name}`). Nodes in the
`skip` set are excluded from traversal — used to skip `ImportDeclaration`
nodes so the import's own specifier identifier doesn't false-positive.

Skips `Identifier` nodes in non-reference positions defined by
`NON_REFERENCE_FIELDS` — for example, `obj.Mdz` (non-computed member property),
`{ Mdz: value }` (non-computed object key), and statement labels.

Safe for Svelte template ASTs: `Component.name` is a plain string property
(not an `Identifier` node), so `<Mdz>` tags do not produce false matches.
declarations.type_signature: (node: unknown, name: string, skip?: Set<unknown> | undefined): boolean
declarations.return_description: `true` if a matching `Identifier` node is found

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: the identifier name to look for

parameters.name: skip
parameters.description: set of AST nodes to skip during traversal

declarations.name: escape_svelte_text
declarations.doc_comment:
Escapes text for safe embedding in Svelte template markup.

Uses a single-pass regex replacement to avoid corruption that occurs with sequential
`.replace()` calls (where the second replace matches characters introduced by the first).

Escapes four characters:
- `{` → `{'{'}` and `}` → `{'}'}` — prevents Svelte expression interpretation
- `<` → `<` — prevents HTML/Svelte tag interpretation
- `&` → `&` — prevents HTML entity interpretation

The `&` escaping is necessary because runtime `MdzNodeView.svelte` renders text
with `{node.content}` (a Svelte expression), which auto-escapes `&` to `&`.
The preprocessor emits raw template text where `&` is NOT auto-escaped, so
manual escaping is required to match the runtime behavior.

declarations.name: remove_variable_declaration
declarations.doc_comment:
Removes a single-declarator `VariableDeclaration` from source using MagicString.

Consumes leading whitespace (tabs/spaces) and trailing newline to avoid leaving
blank lines. Only safe for single-declarator statements (`const x = 'val';`);
callers must verify `node.declarations.length === 1` before calling.
declarations.type_signature: (s: { remove: (start: number, end: number) => unknown; }, declaration_node: VariableDeclaration & { start: number; end: number; }, source: string): void

parameters.name: declaration_node
parameters.description: the `VariableDeclaration` AST node with Svelte position data

declarations.name: remove_import_declaration
declarations.doc_comment:
Removes an `ImportDeclaration` from source using MagicString.

Consumes leading whitespace (tabs/spaces) and trailing newline to avoid leaving
blank lines.
declarations.type_signature: (s: { remove: (start: number, end: number) => unknown; }, import_node: ImportDeclaration & { start: number; end: number; }, source: string): void

parameters.name: import_node
parameters.description: the `ImportDeclaration` AST node with Svelte position data

declarations.name: remove_import_specifier
declarations.doc_comment:
Removes a specifier from a multi-specifier import declaration by
reconstructing the statement without the removed specifier.

Overwrites the entire declaration range to avoid character-level comma surgery.

Handles:
- `import Mdz, {other} from '...'` → `import {other} from '...'`
- `import {default as Mdz, other} from '...'` → `import {other} from '...'`
- `import {Mdz, other} from '...'` → `import {other} from '...'`
declarations.type_signature: (s: { overwrite: (start: number, end: number, content: string) => unknown; }, node: ImportDeclaration & { start: number; end: number; }, specifier_to_remove: ImportSpecifier | ImportDefaultSpecifier | ImportNamespaceSpecifier, source: string, additional_lines?: string): void

parameters.name: s
parameters.type: { overwrite: (start: number, end: number, content: string) => unknown; }

parameters.name: additional_lines
parameters.description:
extra content appended after the reconstructed import
(used to bundle new imports into the overwrite to avoid MagicString boundary conflicts).

declarations.name: handle_preprocess_error
declarations.doc_comment: Handles errors during Svelte preprocessing with configurable behavior.

throws.description: wrapping `error` (with original as `cause`) when `on_error` is `'throw'`

declarations.type_signature: (error: unknown, prefix: string, filename: string | undefined, on_error: "log" | "throw"): void

parameters.name: on_error
parameters.description: `'throw'` to re-throw as a new Error, `'log'` to console.error

modules.module_comment:
Shared helper functions for Svelte preprocessors.

Provides AST utilities for detecting static content, resolving imports,
managing import statements, and escaping strings for Svelte templates.
Used by `svelte_preprocess_mdz` in fuz_ui, `svelte_preprocess_fuz_code`
in fuz_code, and potentially other Svelte preprocessors.

Uses `import type` from `svelte/compiler` for AST types only — no runtime
Svelte dependency. Consumers must have `svelte` installed for type resolution.

declarations.name: assert_property
declarations.doc_comment:
Narrows a discriminated union by a literal property value, failing the test
if the value doesn't match. The assertion signature propagates the narrowed
type so callers can access variant-specific fields directly.

Works with any discriminator key (`kind`, `ok`, `type`, `_tag`, etc.).
declarations.type_signature: <R extends object, P extends keyof R, const V extends R[P]>(obj: R, property: P, value: V): asserts obj is Extract<R, Record<P, V>>

declarations.name: assert_rejects
declarations.doc_comment:
Asserts that `fn` rejects with an `Error`.
Optionally matches the error message against `pattern`.
Returns the caught `Error` for further assertions by the caller.

`assert.fail` is placed after the catch block so that assertion failures
from the test itself are not swallowed by the catch.
declarations.return_description: the caught `Error`

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: optional regex to match against the error message

declarations.name: MockLogger
declarations.doc_comment:
A mock `Logger` with `vi.fn()` methods and call tracking arrays.
Assignable to `Logger` for use in code under test.
Each tracking array captures the first argument of each call.
For full call details, use `vi.fn()` introspection on the methods directly.

declarations.name: create_mock_logger
declarations.doc_comment:
Creates a mock `Logger` with `vi.fn()` on each logging method
and tracking arrays for inspecting logged messages.
Follows the fuz-stack convention of plain object mocks over mocking libraries.

modules.module_comment:
Shared test assertions for the `@fuzdev` ecosystem.

Extends the fuz-stack testing conventions (`assert` from vitest, tests in `src/test/`,
plain object mocks) with reusable helpers for patterns that appear across multiple repos.
Only depends on vitest — safe for fuz_util's zero-runtime-deps constraint.

properties.name: delay
properties.doc_comment: Enforced milliseconds between calls. For `when='trailing'` this is the debounce delay.

properties.name: when
properties.doc_comment: When to call the throttled function. Defaults to `both`.

declarations.name: throttle
declarations.doc_comment:
Throttles calls to a callback that returns a void promise.
Immediately invokes the callback on the first call unless `leading=false`.
If the throttled function is called while the promise is already pending,
the call is queued to run after the pending promise completes plus `delay`,
and only the last call is invoked.
In other words, all calls and their args are discarded
during the pending window except for the most recent.
Unlike debouncing, this calls the throttled callback
both on the leading and trailing edges of the delay window by default,
and this can be customized by setting `when` to `'leading'` or `'trailing'`.
It also differs from a queue where every call to the throttled callback eventually runs.
declarations.type_signature: <T extends (...args: Array<any>) => Promise<void>>(cb: T, { delay, when }?: ThrottleOptions): T
declarations.return_description: same as `cb`

declarations.name: Timer
declarations.doc_comment:
Timer interface for measuring elapsed time.
Returns time in nanoseconds for maximum precision.

properties.name: now
properties.doc_comment: Get current time in nanoseconds

declarations.name: timer_node
declarations.doc_comment:
Node.js high-resolution timer using process.hrtime.bigint().
Provides true nanosecond precision.

declarations.name: timer_browser
declarations.doc_comment:
Browser high-resolution timer using performance.now().
Converts milliseconds to nanoseconds for consistent API.

**Precision varies by browser due to Spectre/Meltdown mitigations:**
- Chrome: ~100μs (coarsened)
- Firefox: ~1ms (rounded)
- Safari: ~100μs
- Node.js: ~1μs

For nanosecond-precision benchmarks, use Node.js with `timer_node`.

declarations.name: timer_default
declarations.doc_comment:
Auto-detected timer based on environment.
Uses process.hrtime in Node.js, performance.now() in browsers.
The timer function is detected once and cached for performance.

declarations.name: TIME_NS_PER_US
declarations.doc_comment: Time units and conversions.

declarations.name: time_ns_to_us
declarations.doc_comment: Convert nanoseconds to microseconds.

declarations.name: time_ns_to_ms
declarations.doc_comment: Convert nanoseconds to milliseconds.

declarations.name: time_ns_to_sec
declarations.doc_comment: Convert nanoseconds to seconds.

declarations.name: TimeUnit
declarations.doc_comment: Time unit for formatting.

declarations.name: TIME_UNIT_DISPLAY
declarations.doc_comment: Display labels for time units (uses proper Unicode μ for microseconds).

declarations.name: time_unit_detect_best
declarations.doc_comment:
Detect the best time unit for a set of nanosecond values.
Chooses the unit where most values fall in the range 1-9999.

declarations.name: time_format
declarations.doc_comment: Format time with a specific unit.
declarations.type_signature: (ns: number, unit: TimeUnit, decimals?: number): string
declarations.return_description: formatted string like "3.87μs"

declarations.name: time_format_adaptive
declarations.doc_comment: Format time with adaptive units (ns/μs/ms/s) based on magnitude.
examples:
```ts
time_format_adaptive(1500) // "1.50μs"
time_format_adaptive(3870) // "3.87μs"
time_format_adaptive(1500000) // "1.50ms"
time_format_adaptive(1500000000) // "1.50s"
```
declarations.return_description: formatted string like "3.87μs" or "1.23ms"

declarations.name: TimeResult
declarations.doc_comment:
Result from timing a function execution.
All times in nanoseconds for maximum precision.

properties.name: elapsed_ns
properties.doc_comment: Elapsed time in nanoseconds

properties.name: elapsed_us
properties.doc_comment: Elapsed time in microseconds (convenience)

properties.name: elapsed_ms
properties.doc_comment: Elapsed time in milliseconds (convenience)

properties.name: started_at_ns
properties.doc_comment: Start time in nanoseconds (from timer.now())

properties.name: ended_at_ns
properties.doc_comment: End time in nanoseconds (from timer.now())

declarations.name: time_async
declarations.doc_comment: Time an asynchronous function execution.
declarations.type_signature: <T>(fn: () => Promise<T>, timer?: Timer): Promise<{ result: T; timing: TimeResult; }>

declarations.name: time_sync
declarations.doc_comment: Time a synchronous function execution.
declarations.type_signature: <T>(fn: () => T, timer?: Timer): { result: T; timing: TimeResult; }

Provides cross-platform high-resolution timing and measurement helpers.

Tracks and manages multiple timing operations.
Allows starting, stopping, and retrieving timings with optional precision.

declarations.name: PickUnion
declarations.doc_comment: Like `Pick` but works for type unions.

declarations.name: KeyofUnion
declarations.doc_comment: Like `keyof` but works for type unions.

declarations.name: Flavored
declarations.doc_comment:
The `Flavored` and `Branded` type helpers add varying degrees of nominal typing to other types.
This is especially useful with primitives like strings and numbers.
see_also: https://spin.atomicobject.com/typescript-flexible-nominal-typing/ * `Flavored` is a looser form of `Branded` that trades explicitness and a little safety in some cases for ergonomics. With `Flavored` you don't need to cast unflavored types: * ```ts type Email = Flavored<string, 'Email'>; const email1: Email = 'foo'; // ok type Address = Flavored<string, 'Address'>; const email2: Email = 'foo' as Address; // error! ``` * `Branded` requires casting: * ```ts type PhoneNumber = Branded<string, 'PhoneNumber'>; const phone1: PhoneNumber = 'foo'; // error! const phone2: PhoneNumber = 'foo' as PhoneNumber; // ok ``` * See also Zod's `.brand` schema helper:

declarations.name: format_url
declarations.doc_comment:
Formats a URL by removing 'https://', 'www.', and trailing slashes.
Notably it does not remove 'http://', so the user can see that it's insecure.

declarations.name: zod_to_subschema

declarations.return_description: inner schema if wrapped, undefined otherwise

declarations.name: ZOD_WRAPPER_TYPES
declarations.doc_comment: Zod wrapper type names that `zod_unwrap_def` traverses through.

declarations.name: zod_unwrap_def
declarations.doc_comment:
Unwrap Zod wrappers (optional, default, nullable, pipe, transform)
to get the base type definition.
declarations.return_description: the innermost non-wrapper type definition

declarations.name: zod_get_base_type
declarations.doc_comment: Get the base type name for a Zod schema, unwrapping all wrappers.
declarations.return_description: base type name (e.g. `'string'`, `'object'`, `'uuid'`)

declarations.name: zod_is_optional
declarations.doc_comment: Check if a schema is optional at the outermost level.

declarations.name: zod_is_nullable
declarations.doc_comment: Check if a schema accepts null at any wrapping level.

declarations.name: zod_has_default
declarations.doc_comment:
Check if a schema has a default value at any wrapping level.

Unlike `zod_to_schema_default` (which returns the value), this returns a boolean.
Distinguishes "no default" from "default is undefined".

declarations.name: zod_get_innermost_type
declarations.doc_comment:
Unwrap a schema through every wrapper type (optional, nullable, default,
transform, pipe, prefault) to reach the innermost schema. Like `zod_unwrap_def`
but returns the schema (so callers can `.shape`, `instanceof`-check, etc.)
instead of the def.
declarations.type_signature: (schema: ZodType<unknown, unknown, $ZodTypeInternals<unknown, unknown>>): ZodType<unknown, unknown, $ZodTypeInternals<unknown, unknown>>
declarations.return_description: the innermost non-wrapper schema

declarations.name: zod_get_schema_keys
declarations.doc_comment:
Get all property keys from an object schema, unwrapping wrappers.
Returns an empty array if the innermost type is not an object.

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: Zod schema (typically a `ZodObject` with possible wrappers)

Get the field schema for a key in an object schema, returning `undefined` if
the schema is not an object or the key is missing.

parameters.name: schema

Unwrap a schema to find the inner `ZodObject`, or `null` if not an object schema.

modules.module_comment:
Zod schema introspection utilities.

Generic helpers for walking, unwrapping, and extracting metadata from Zod schemas.
Used by CLI argument parsing, adversarial input testing, and surface generation.

gro.name: @fuzdev/gro
gro.logo_alt: a pixelated green oak acorn with a glint of sun

package_json.name: @fuzdev/gro
package_json.description: task runner and toolkit extending SvelteKit
package_json.logo_alt: a pixelated green oak acorn with a glint of sun

scripts.bootstrap: rm -rf .gro dist && svelte-kit sync && svelte-package && chmod +x ./dist/gro.js && npm link -f

declarations.name: to_task_args
declarations.doc_comment:
Parses `task_name` and `args` from `process.argv`,
ignoring anything after any `--`.

declarations.name: to_raw_rest_args
declarations.doc_comment: Gets the array of raw string args starting with the first `--`, if any.

declarations.name: to_forwarded_args
declarations.doc_comment:
Parses `process.argv` for the specified `command`, so given
`gro taskname arg1 --arg2 -- eslint eslintarg1 --eslintarg2 -- tsc --tscarg1 --tscarg2`
the `command` `'eslint'` returns `eslintarg1 --eslintarg2`
and `'tsc'` returns `--tscarg1` and `--tscarg2`.
declarations.type_signature: (command: string, raw_rest_args?: string[] | undefined, cache?: Record<string, Args | undefined>): Args

declarations.name: to_implicit_forwarded_args
declarations.doc_comment:
Gets all args after the first `--` without assuming a command name.
This is useful for tasks that want to forward args directly to a tool
without requiring users to specify the tool name explicitly.
Optionally strips a specific command name if present for backward compatibility.

declarations.name: BuildOutputEntry

Metadata about a single build output file.
Includes cryptographic hash for validation plus filesystem stats for debugging and optimization.

Metadata stored in .gro/ directory to track build cache validity.

declarations.doc_comment:
Computes the cache key components for a build.
This determines whether a cached build can be reused.

parameters.description: Gro config (`build_cache_config_hash` is already computed during config load)

parameters.name: git_commit
parameters.description: optional pre-computed git commit hash (optimization to avoid re-reading)

declarations.doc_comment:
Loads build cache metadata from .gro/ directory.
Invalid or corrupted cache files are automatically deleted.

Saves build cache metadata to .gro/ directory.
Errors are logged but don't fail the build (cache is optional).

parameters.type: { version: string; git_commit: string | null; build_cache_config_hash: string; timestamp: string; outputs: { path: string; hash: string; size: number; mtime: number; ctime: number; mode: number; }[]; }

declarations.name: validate_build_cache
declarations.doc_comment:
Validates that a cached build is still valid by checking stats and hashing outputs.
Uses size as a fast negative check before expensive hashing.
This is comprehensive validation to catch manual tampering or corruption.
declarations.type_signature: (metadata: { version: string; git_commit: string | null; build_cache_config_hash: string; timestamp: string; outputs: { path: string; hash: string; size: number; mtime: number; ctime: number; mode: number; }[]; }): Promise<...>

parameters.name: metadata

Main function to check if the build cache is valid.
Returns true if the cached build can be used, false if a fresh build is needed.

declarations.name: collect_build_outputs
declarations.doc_comment:
Collects information about all files in build output directories.
Returns an array of entries with path, hash, size, mtime, ctime, and mode.

Files are hashed in parallel for performance. For very large builds (10k+ files),
this may take several seconds but ensures complete cache validation.

Discovers all build output directories in the current working directory.
Returns an array of directory names that exist: build/, dist/, dist_*

declarations.name: create_build_cache_metadata

Creates build cache metadata after a successful build.
Automatically discovers all build output directories (build/, dist/, dist_*).

This may be better implemented as a standalone dependency

BLAKE3 hash of empty string, used for configs without `build_cache_config`.
This ensures consistent cache behavior when no custom config is provided.

declarations.name: GroConfig
declarations.doc_comment:
The config that users can extend via `gro.config.ts`.
This is exposed to users in places like tasks and genfiles.

properties.name: map_package_json
properties.doc_comment:
Maps the project's `package.json` before writing it to the filesystem.
The `package_json` argument may be mutated, but the return value is what's used by the caller.
Returning `null` is a no-op for the caller.

properties.name: task_root_dirs
properties.doc_comment:
The root directories to search for tasks given implicit relative input paths.
Defaults to `./src/lib`, then the cwd, then the Gro package dist.

properties.name: search_filters
properties.doc_comment:
When searching the filesystem for tasks and genfiles,
directories and files are included if they pass all of these filters.

properties.name: js_cli
properties.doc_comment: The CLI to use that's compatible with `node`.

properties.name: pm_cli
properties.doc_comment: The CLI to use that's compatible with `npm install` and `npm link`. Defaults to `'npm'`.

properties.name: build_cache_config_hash
properties.doc_comment:
SHA-256 hash of the user's `build_cache_config` from `gro.config.ts`.
This is computed during config normalization and the raw value is immediately deleted.
If no `build_cache_config` was provided, this is the hash of an empty string.

properties.name: filer_options
properties.doc_comment: Options passed to the `Filer` for file watching and import resolution.

declarations.name: RawGroConfig
declarations.doc_comment:
The relaxed variant of `GroConfig` that users can provide via `gro.config.ts`.
Superset of `GroConfig`.

properties.name: build_cache_config
properties.type_signature:
| Record<string, unknown>
		| (() => Record<string, unknown> | Promise<Record<string, unknown>>)
properties.doc_comment:
Optional object defining custom build inputs for cache invalidation.
This value is hashed during config normalization and used to detect
when builds need to be regenerated due to non-source changes.

Use cases:
- Environment variables baked into build: `{api_url: process.env.PUBLIC_API_URL}`
- External data files: `{data: fs.readFileSync('data.json', 'utf-8')}`
- Build feature flags: `{enable_analytics: true}`

Can be a static object or an async function that returns an object.

IMPORTANT: It's safe to include secrets here because they are hashed and `delete`d
during config normalization. The raw value is never logged or persisted.

properties.name: filer_options
properties.doc_comment: Options passed to the `Filer` for file watching and import resolution.

declarations.name: SEARCH_EXCLUDER_DEFAULT
declarations.doc_comment:
The regexp used by default to exclude directories and files
when searching the filesystem for tasks and genfiles.
Customize via `search_filters` in the `GroConfig`.
See the test cases for the exact behavior.

declarations.name: cook_gro_config
declarations.doc_comment:
Transforms a `RawGroConfig` to the more strict `GroConfig`.
This allows users to provide a more relaxed config.
Hashes the `build_cache_config` and deletes the raw value for security.

declarations.name: validate_gro_config_module
declarations.type_signature: (config_module: any, config_path: string): asserts config_module is GroConfigModule

declarations.name: resolve_gro_module_path
declarations.doc_comment:
Resolves a path to an internal Gro file.
Prefers any local installation of Gro and falls back to the current CLI context.

Uses heuristics to find `path`, so may fail in some rare corner cases.
Currently looks for `gro.ts` as a sibling to the `path` arg for detection.
If this fails for your usecases, rename `gro.ts` or open an issue/PR!

Used by the CLI and `gro run`.

case 1:

We're in a directory that has a local installation of Gro at `node_modules/.bin/gro`.
Use this local version instead of the global.

case 2:

We're running Gro inside the Gro repo itself.

In this case, we use the build directory instead of dist.
There's a paradox here for using Gro inside itself -
ideally we use the dist directory because that's what's shipped,
but the build directory has all of the tests,
and loading two instances of its modules causes problems
like `instanceof` checks failing.
For now we'll just run from build and see if it causes any problems.
There's probably a better design in here somewhere.

case 3:

Fall back to invoking Gro from wherever the CLI is being executed.
When using the global CLI, this uses the global Gro installation.

declarations.name: spawn_with_loader
declarations.doc_comment:
Runs a file using the Gro loader.

Uses conditional exports to correctly set up `esm-env` as development by default,
so if you want production set `NODE_ENV=production`.
declarations.type_signature: (loader_path: string, invoke_path: string, argv: string[], js_cli?: string): Promise<SpawnResult>

declarations.name: gro_plugin_deno_compile

Creates a gro plugin that compiles TypeScript to a binary using `deno compile`.
Runs during the `adapt` phase (production build only).

modules.module_comment:
Gro build plugin for compiling TypeScript to a standalone binary via `deno compile`.

Runs during the `adapt` phase (production build only).
Optionally generates a SHA-256 hash file for verification.

To avoid bundling all npm devDependencies from `package.json`, we:
1. Extract just the "imports" from root deno.json
2. Rebase relative paths (e.g. `../fuz_app/` → `../../fuz_app/`) since
   compilation runs from the output subdirectory
3. Write a minimal deno.json to the output directory
4. Compile from that directory (which has no `package.json`)

The server is started during setup and stopped on teardown.

In dev mode, Vite should proxy API requests to this server.

modules.module_comment:
Gro plugin for running a Deno server alongside the Vite dev server.

Unlike `gro_plugin_server`, this doesn't use esbuild bundling since
Deno can run TypeScript directly with JSR imports.

so the build and dev tasks can be the source of truth for `dev`.

properties.name: rebuild_throttle_delay

Milliseconds to throttle rebuilds.

properties.name: cli_command

The CLI command to run the server, like `'node'` or `'bun'` or `'deno'`.

declarations.name: to_input_path
declarations.doc_comment:
Raw input paths are paths that users provide to Gro to reference files for tasks and gen.

A raw input path can be to a file or directory in the following forms:

- an absolute path, preserved
- an explicit relative path, e.g. `./src/foo`, resolved to `root_path` which defaults to the cwd
- an implicit relative path, e.g. `src/foo`, preserved
- an implicit relative path prefixed with `gro/`, transformed to absolute in the Gro directory

Thus, input paths are either absolute or implicitly relative.

declarations.name: get_possible_paths
declarations.doc_comment:
Gets a list of possible source ids for each input path with `extensions`,
duplicating each under `root_dirs`, without checking the filesystem.
declarations.type_signature: (input_path: InputPath, root_dirs: PathId[], extensions: string[]): Promise<PossiblePath[]>

declarations.name: resolve_input_paths
declarations.doc_comment:
Gets the path data for each input path, checking the filesystem for the possibilities
and stopping at the first existing file or falling back to the first existing directory.
If none is found for an input path, it's added to `unmapped_input_paths`.
declarations.type_signature: (input_paths: InputPath[], root_dirs: PathId[], extensions: string[]): Promise<ResolvedInputPaths>

declarations.name: resolve_input_files
declarations.doc_comment:
Finds all of the matching files for the given input paths.
De-dupes source ids.

declarations.name: invoke_task
declarations.doc_comment:
Invokes Gro tasks by name using the filesystem as the source.

When a task is invoked,
Gro first searches for tasks in the current working directory.
and falls back to searching Gro's directory, if the two are different.
See `src/lib/input_path.ts` for info about what "task_name" can refer to.
If it matches a directory, all of the tasks within it are logged,
both in the current working directory and Gro.

This code is particularly hairy because
we're accepting a wide range of user input
and trying to do the right thing.
Precise error messages are especially difficult and
there are some subtle differences in the complex logical branches.
The comments describe each condition.
declarations.type_signature: (task_name: RawInputPath, args: Args | undefined, config: GroConfig, initial_filer?: Filer | undefined, initial_timings?: Timings | null | undefined, parent_log?: Logger | undefined): Promise<...>

The `base` and `assets` in particular are renamed for clarity with Gro's internal systems,
so these properties become first-class vocabulary inside Gro.

Class-to-variable index for bundled CSS generation.

Builds an index mapping CSS class names to the CSS variables they use.
This enables the bundled generator to include theme variables needed
by the utility classes actually used in the project.

declarations.name: resolve_css
declarations.doc_comment:
Resolves which CSS to include based on detected usage.

Algorithm:
1. Collect rules: core rules + element-matching + class-matching
2. Collect variables: from rules, class defs, utility generation, detected vars, additional_variables
3. Resolve transitive variable dependencies
4. Generate output: theme_css (light+dark), base_css (source order)
declarations.return_description: resolution result with theme CSS, base CSS, and diagnostics

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: resolution options including indexes, detected usage, and config

declarations.doc_comment: Options for bundled CSS output generation.

properties.name: include_theme
properties.doc_comment: Include theme variables section.

properties.name: include_base

properties.name: include_utilities
properties.doc_comment: Include utility classes section.

declarations.doc_comment: Generates combined CSS output from resolution result.
declarations.type_signature: (result: CssResolutionResult, utility_css: string, options?: GenerateBundledCssOptions): string
declarations.return_description: combined CSS string

modules.module_comment:
CSS resolution algorithm for bundled CSS generation.

Combines theme variables, base styles, and utility classes into a single
bundled output. Resolves which CSS rules and variables to include based
on what elements and classes are actually used in the project.

This module provides low-level CSS resolution for advanced use cases.
Most users should use `gen_fuz_css` (Gro) or `vite_plugin_fuz_css` (Vite)
which handle extraction and caching automatically. See those modules for
reference implementations.

declarations.name: DEFAULT_CACHE_DIR
declarations.doc_comment: Default cache directory relative to project root.

declarations.name: CSS_CACHE_VERSION
declarations.doc_comment:
CSS cache version. Bump when any of these change:
- `CachedExtraction` schema
- `extract_css_classes_with_locations()` logic or output
- `ExtractionDiagnostic` or `SourceLocation` structure

v1: Initial version with classes and diagnostics
v2: Use null instead of empty arrays, add `explicit_classes`, `elements`, `css_variables`.
v3: Add `explicit_elements`, `explicit_variables` for `@fuz-elements`/`@fuz-variables` comments.
v4: Filter incomplete CSS variables in dynamic templates (e.g., `var(--prefix_{expr})`).
v5: Remove `css_variables` and `explicit_variables` (now detected via simple regex scan).
v6: Re-add `explicit_variables` for `@fuz-variables` comments (regex scan misses dynamic templates).

declarations.name: ExtractionResult
declarations.doc_comment:
Extraction result with classes mapped to their source locations.
Extends `ExtractionData` with `tracked_vars` which is internal to AST walking.

Uses embedded diagnostics (rather than a Result type) because file extraction
can partially succeed: some classes may be extracted while others produce errors.
This differs from `CssLiteralParseResult` which uses a discriminated union
because single-class parsing is binary success/failure.

Helper class for converting character offsets to line/column positions.
Svelte template nodes (Comment, Text, ExpressionTag) only have char offsets,
so this class enables efficient conversion.

Build: O(n) where n = source length
Lookup: O(log m) where m = number of lines (binary search)

modules.module_comment:
AST-based CSS class extraction for Svelte and TypeScript files.

Replaces regex-based extraction with proper parsing to handle:
- `class="display:flex"` - string attributes
- `class={{ active, disabled: !enabled }}` - object attributes (Svelte 5.16+)
- `class={[cond && 'box', 'display:flex']}` - array attributes (Svelte 5.16+)
- `class:active={cond}` - class directives
- `clsx('foo', { bar: true })` - class utility function calls
- Variables with class-related names
- `// @fuz-classes class1 class2` - comment hints for dynamic classes
- `// @fuz-elements element1 element2` - comment hints for dynamic elements
- `// @fuz-variables var1 var2` - comment hints for dynamic CSS variables

declarations.name: escape_css_selector
declarations.doc_comment:
Escapes special characters in a CSS class name for use in a selector.
CSS selectors require escaping of characters like `:`, `%`, `(`, `)`, etc.
examples:
```ts
escape_css_selector('display:flex') // 'display\\:flex'
escape_css_selector('opacity:80%') // 'opacity\\:80\\%'
escape_css_selector('nth-child(2n)') // 'nth-child\\(2n\\)'
```

declarations.name: extract_primary_property
declarations.doc_comment:
Extracts the CSS property name from a single-property declaration string.
Returns `null` for multi-property declarations (composites with multiple semicolons).
examples:
```ts
extract_primary_property('border-radius: var(--border_radius_sm);') // 'border-radius'
extract_primary_property('display: flex; align-items: center;') // null
```

declarations.name: CssClassDefinitionDeclaration

Classes that were explicitly annotated (via `@fuz-classes` or `additional_classes`).
