declarations.name: resolve_env_vars
declarations.doc_comment:
Resolve environment variable references in a string.

- `$$VAR$$` resolves from the runtime env; missing values are left as-is
  for the validation phase to report.
- `$$?VAR$$` is the optional form — missing or empty resolves to the empty
  string. Required validation skips refs marked optional.
- `\$$VAR$$` / `\$$?VAR$$` are escapes — the leading backslash is dropped
  and the body is emitted literally (no resolution attempted).
declarations.return_description: string with env vars resolved

declarations.name: has_env_vars
declarations.doc_comment:
Check if a string contains unresolved env var references.

Escaped references (`\$$VAR$$`) do not count — they're literal text once
resolved.
declarations.return_description: `true` if string contains unescaped `$$VAR$$` patterns

declarations.name: get_env_var_names
declarations.doc_comment:
Get list of env var names referenced in a string.

Escaped references are skipped; optional and required references are both
included (callers that care about the distinction should use `scan_env_vars`
which preserves the `optional` flag per ref).
declarations.return_description: array of variable names (without `$$` delimiters)

declarations.name: resolve_env_vars_in_object
declarations.doc_comment: Resolve env vars in an object's string values (shallow).
declarations.type_signature: <T extends Record<string, unknown>>(runtime: Pick<EnvDeps, "env_get">, obj: T): T
declarations.return_description: new object with env vars resolved

declarations.name: resolve_env_vars_required
declarations.doc_comment:
Resolve env vars and throw if any are missing/empty.

Use this for values that must be present. `$$?VAR$$` (optional) refs
resolve to the empty string on miss without contributing to the error.
Escaped references (`\$$VAR$$`) emit literally and never check the env.

throws.description: if any referenced env var is missing or empty

declarations.type_signature: (runtime: Pick<EnvDeps, "env_get">, value: string, context: string): string
declarations.return_description: resolved string

declarations.name: EnvVarRef
declarations.doc_comment: An env var reference found in a config.

properties.name: name
properties.doc_comment: Variable name (without `$$` delimiters).

properties.name: path
properties.doc_comment: Path where the reference was found (e.g., `"target.host"`, `"resources[3].path"`).

properties.name: optional
properties.doc_comment:
Whether the reference is optional (`$$?VAR$$`). Optional refs resolve
to the empty string when unset and are skipped by `validate_env_vars` —
a var that's intentionally blank doesn't count as missing.

declarations.name: scan_env_vars
declarations.doc_comment:
Recursively scan an object for `$$VAR$$` env var references.

Walks all string values in the object tree and extracts env var names
with their path context for error reporting. Escaped references
(`\$$VAR$$`) are skipped — they're literal text, not references.
The `optional` flag on each ref distinguishes `$$VAR$$` (required) from
`$$?VAR$$` (optional) for downstream validation.
declarations.return_description: array of env var references with paths and optional flags

declarations.name: EnvValidationResult
declarations.doc_comment:
Result of env var validation.

Uses discriminated union for better type narrowing:
- `ok: true, missing: null` — all vars present
- `ok: false, missing: EnvVarRef[]` — some vars missing

declarations.name: validate_env_vars
declarations.doc_comment:
Validate that all referenced env vars exist in the environment.

Returns all missing refs (including duplicates by name). Grouping
and deduplication is handled by `format_missing_env_vars` at display time.
Refs marked `optional: true` (from `$$?VAR$$` syntax) are skipped — a
deliberately-blank var is contract, not a missing dependency.
declarations.return_description: validation result with any missing vars

declarations.name: FormatMissingEnvVarsOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Options for `format_missing_env_vars`.

properties.name: env_file
properties.doc_comment: Path to env file if one was loaded.

properties.name: setup_hint
properties.doc_comment: Hint text for how to set up the environment.

declarations.name: format_missing_env_vars
declarations.doc_comment:
Format missing env vars error message.

Groups refs by variable name so each missing var is shown once
with all paths where it's referenced.
declarations.return_description: formatted error message for display

parameters.name: missing
parameters.description: missing env var references (may contain duplicate names)

modules.module_comment:
Environment variable `$$VAR$$` resolution suite.

Resolves `$$VAR$$` references in strings and object trees,
scans configs for references, and validates/formats missing vars.

The double-dollar bookending syntax is:
- Visually distinct from shell `$VAR` syntax
- Unambiguous about variable boundaries
- Easy to grep: `grep '\$\$'`
- Fails loud if accidentally shell-processed (`$$`=PID in shell)

# Syntax

- `$$VAR$$` — required reference. Missing or empty fails validation
  (see `validate_env_vars`).
- `$$?VAR$$` — optional reference. Missing or empty resolves to the
  empty string and passes validation. Use for vars that exist in the
  contract but may be intentionally blank (e.g. `SMTP_PASSWORD=` on
  a deployment that hasn't configured SMTP yet).
- `\$$VAR$$` — escape. The leading backslash is dropped at resolution
  time and the rest is emitted literally. Use this when documenting
  the syntax inside a string literal (comments in env-file templates,
  for example) where a regex scan would otherwise treat the mention
  as a real reference. Combines with `?` as `\$$?VAR$$`.

declarations.name: UpdateEnvVariableOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Options for updating environment variables in a .env file.

properties.name: env_file_path
properties.doc_comment: Path to the .env file.

properties.name: read_file
properties.doc_comment: Function to read file contents (defaults to `node:fs/promises` `readFile`).

properties.name: write_file
properties.type_signature: (path: string, content: string, encoding: string) => Promise<void>
properties.doc_comment: Function to write file contents (defaults to `node:fs/promises` `writeFile`).

declarations.name: update_env_variable
declarations.doc_comment:
Updates or adds an environment variable in the .env file.
Preserves existing formatting, comments, and other variables.

Behavior:
- **Duplicate keys**: updates the LAST occurrence (matches dotenv behavior)
- **Inline comments**: preserved after the value (e.g., `KEY=value # comment`)
- **Quote style**: preserved from original (quoted/unquoted)

throws.description: if the file read fails for any reason other than `ENOENT`, or if the write fails

declarations.type_signature: (key: string, value: string, options: UpdateEnvVariableOptions): Promise<void>

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: the new value for the environment variable

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: file path and optional read/write overrides

modules.module_comment: Write updates to `.env` files while preserving formatting.

declarations.name: CREDENTIAL_TYPES
declarations.doc_comment:
The credential types that can authenticate a request — the closed set
of fuz_app builtins. The open registry on top
(`create_credential_type_schema(consumer_types)`) is consulted at
registry time by `create_role_schema` for `RoleSpec.required_credential_types`
validation; the wire-validated `CredentialType` enum here stays
narrow because middleware only ever sets one of the three builtins.

declarations.name: CredentialType
declarations.doc_comment: Credential type — how a request was authenticated.

declarations.name: CREDENTIAL_TYPE_KEY
declarations.doc_comment: Hono context variable name for the credential type.

declarations.name: AUTH_API_TOKEN_ID_KEY
declarations.doc_comment: Hono context variable name for the authenticated API token id.

declarations.name: ACCOUNT_ID_KEY
declarations.doc_comment:
Hono context variable name for the authenticated account id.

Set by the auth middleware (session, bearer, or daemon token) on a valid
credential. `null` for unauthenticated requests. The route-spec wrapper /
RPC dispatcher's authorization phase reads this when resolving the acting
actor; account-grain auth guards (`require_auth`) and account-grain handlers
read it directly.

declarations.name: TEST_CONTEXT_PRESET_KEY
declarations.doc_comment:
Hono context variable name for the test-harness pre-baked context flag.

Test harnesses (`create_test_app_from_specs`, `create_fake_hono_context`,
the WS round-trip `connect()` helper, plus per-test middleware that
pre-populates `REQUEST_CONTEXT_KEY`) set this to `true` so
`apply_authorization_phase` skips its DB-backed actor resolution and
trusts the supplied `RequestContext`. Production middleware never sets
this key — only test code does. The flag is the explicit escape hatch
that replaced the implicit "is `REQUEST_CONTEXT_KEY` already set?" probe,
so that future production code consulting `REQUEST_CONTEXT_KEY` cannot
silently bypass the live build.

modules.module_comment:
Hono context variable augmentation for fuz_app.

Cross-cutting shared vocabulary — defines the Hono `ContextVariableMap`
variables used by auth, http, server, and testing modules.

Auto-loaded by `server/app_server.ts` (side-effect import) and
transitively by auth middleware modules that import `CREDENTIAL_TYPE_KEY`.
Consumers don't need a manual import unless bypassing the standard server assembly.

declarations.name: ActingActor
declarations.doc_comment:
`acting` field shared by every input that needs the caller's acting actor.
Declaring `acting: ActingActor` on a route or action input signals to the
dispatcher's authorization phase to resolve an actor against the
authenticated account: it runs `resolve_acting_actor`, builds the
actor-bound `RequestContext`, and loads role_grants before auth guards fire.

Resolution rules: omitted + 1 actor → use it; omitted + multiple actors →
`actor_required` with the available list; supplied + on the account → use
it; supplied + foreign actor → `actor_not_on_account`.

Account-grain routes — input doesn't declare `acting` and auth doesn't
require role_grants — skip resolution entirely; their `RequestContext.actor`
is `null` and the audit envelope's `actor_id` stays null.

Lives next to `RouteAuth` because the two are paired by registry-time
invariant 2: `auth.actor !== 'none'` ⟺ input (or query, on REST GETs)
declares `acting?: ActingActor`. Keeping the contract in one module
removes the http/ → auth/ import that an earlier split forced.

declarations.name: AuthAxisState
declarations.doc_comment:
Per-axis auth state — names the dispatcher's behavior on `account` and
`actor` independently:

- `'none'` — explicitly skipped, even when the credential provides it.
  Public actions (no auth surface) and notifications declare this.
- `'optional'` — surfaced if the credential provides it, null otherwise.
  Identity-aware reads with anonymous fallback (cell_get-style) declare
  this on `account` / `actor`.
- `'required'` — must be resolved; the dispatcher rejects requests that
  fail to provide it (401 for `account === 'required'` without a
  credential; the authorization phase 4xx for `actor === 'required'`
  without an actor binding).

declarations.name: RouteAuth
declarations.doc_comment:
The canonical four-axis auth shape used by both `ActionSpec.auth` and
`RouteSpec.auth`.

Cross-axis registry invariants enforced via `.superRefine`:

1. **Roles imply actor.** `roles?.length` ⟹ `actor === 'required'`.
   Role checks read the actor's role_grants, so a role-gated spec without
   a resolved actor would have nothing to check.
3. **No accountless actors yet.** `account === 'none' && actor !== 'none'`
   is invalid in v1. The credential resolver always binds account before
   actor today; agent-token / group-actor credentials will lift this.
4. **Unrestricted is leaf.** `account === 'none' && actor === 'none'`
   ⟹ no `roles`, no `credential_types` (nothing left to gate).

Invariant 2 — the `actor !== 'none' ⟺ input or query declares
acting?: ActingActor` biconditional — needs introspection of the
spec's input/query schemas, so it is checked at registration time,
not on this schema. See `assert_route_auth_acting_biconditional`
below.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ account: ZodEnum<{ none: "none"; optional: "optional"; required: "required"; }>; actor: ZodEnum<{ none: "none"; optional: "optional"; required: "required"; }>; roles: ZodOptional<ZodReadonly<...>>; credential_types: ZodOptional<...>; }, $strict>

declarations.name: is_public_auth
declarations.doc_comment:
True iff the route is fully public — both account and actor axes
are `'none'`. Public routes skip the dispatcher's authorization
phase entirely (per registry-time invariant 4 they also cannot
declare roles or credential gates).
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: needs_actor
declarations.doc_comment:
True iff the route declares an actor axis (`'optional'` or
`'required'`). Equivalent to "the dispatcher's authorization phase
may resolve an actor for this request" — which by registry-time
invariant 2 also means the input (or query, on REST GETs) declares
`acting?: ActingActor`.
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: needs_account
declarations.doc_comment:
True iff the route declares an account axis (`'optional'` or
`'required'`). Per registry-time invariant 3 this is implied by
`needs_actor(auth)` in v1 (no accountless actors yet).
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: is_role_auth
declarations.doc_comment: True iff the route declares any role gate (`auth.roles?.length`).
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: is_credential_gated_auth
declarations.doc_comment: True iff the route declares any credential-type gate (`auth.credential_types?.length`).
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: is_keeper_auth
declarations.doc_comment:
True iff the route is the keeper bucket — credential gate restricted
to `daemon_token`. Keeper is the only credential gate today; if more
land, this filter widens. Knows the `'daemon_token'` literal directly
(the keeper composition is fuz_app's only registered credential gate).
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: is_plain_authenticated_auth
declarations.doc_comment:
True iff the route is plain authenticated — `account === 'required'`
with no role gate and no credential gate. Account-grain authenticated
routes (logout, password change, account self-service) fall here.
declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }): boolean

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

declarations.name: input_schema_declares_acting
declarations.doc_comment:
Whether a schema declares the canonical `acting?: ActingActor` field.
Reference-equality on the exported `ActingActor` schema — consumer
schemas with unrelated `acting` fields don't trip this check.

Peels through Zod wrappers (`optional`, `nullable`, `default`,
`transform`, `pipe`, `prefault`) via `zod_unwrap_to_object` so a spec
authored as `z.optional(z.strictObject({acting: ActingActor}))` or
`z.strictObject({acting: ActingActor}).default({})` still trips the
predicate.

declarations.name: ActingSlots
declarations.doc_comment:
Slots where a spec may declare the `acting?: ActingActor` field —
input for both REST + actions; query for REST GETs that bi-locate
`acting` on the query schema (actions have no `query` shape, so the
field is omitted on action call sites).

declarations.name: assert_route_auth_acting_biconditional
declarations.doc_comment:
Registry-time biconditional check: `auth.actor !== 'none' ⟺ some
supplied slot declares acting?: ActingActor`. Throws on violation.

The slot set differs by surface: REST passes `{input, query}` (both
locatable, query only set for GETs); action dispatchers pass `{input}`
(no query shape on `ActionSpec`). The throw message lists the slots
that were actually in play, so an actor-required action without
`acting` doesn't point the operator at a query slot that doesn't
exist on their spec.

Called by every dispatcher registration loop (`apply_route_specs`,
`compile_action_registry`) on every spec it accepts.

throws.description: when the biconditional is violated

declarations.type_signature: (auth: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }, slots: ActingSlots, context: string): void

parameters.name: auth
parameters.type: { account: "none" | "optional" | "required"; actor: "none" | "optional" | "required"; roles?: readonly string[] | undefined; credential_types?: readonly string[] | undefined; }

parameters.name: slots
parameters.description: the spec's `acting`-bearing schemas; `query` omitted on action call sites

parameters.name: context
parameters.description: identifier for the throwing message (route key, RPC method, etc.)

modules.module_comment:
Canonical four-axis auth shape for action specs and route specs.

Replaces the discriminated `'public' | 'authenticated' | 'keeper' | {role}`
literal that conflated authentication, account resolution, actor resolution,
and authorization into a single value. The flat record names each axis
the dispatcher actually walks:

- `account` — does the dispatcher require / load / skip the account?
- `actor` — does the dispatcher require / load / skip the acting actor?
- `roles` — disjunction of permitted roles (any-of); absent = no role check.
- `credential_types` — restricts the credential channel (e.g. daemon_token);
  absent = any authenticated credential.

The same shape governs both `ActionSpec.auth` (in `actions/action_spec.ts`)
and `RouteSpec.auth` (in `http/route_spec.ts`). The canonical schema
lives here in `http/` because that preserves the existing
`actions → http` dependency direction (and `error_schemas.ts` /
`surface.ts` consume the type).

Registry-time invariants 1, 3, and 4 live on the schema's
`.superRefine` so any spec that fails them throws at the Zod parse
boundary. Invariant 2 (the `actor !== 'none' ⟺ input or query
declares acting?: ActingActor` biconditional) needs introspection of
the spec's input/query schemas, so it is enforced at registration
time inside the dispatcher loops (`apply_route_specs`,
`create_rpc_endpoint`, `register_action_ws`) via the
`assert_route_auth_acting_biconditional` helper exported below.

declarations.name: create_health_route_spec
declarations.doc_comment:
Create a public health check route spec.

Infrastructure endpoint for uptime monitors and load balancers.
Bootstrap availability is exposed via `/api/account/status` instead.

declarations.name: ServerStatusOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Options for the authenticated server status route.

properties.name: version
properties.doc_comment: Application version string.

properties.name: get_uptime_ms
properties.doc_comment: Returns milliseconds since server start.

declarations.name: create_server_status_route_spec
declarations.doc_comment:
Create an authenticated server status route spec.

Returns version and uptime. Unlike the public health check,
this requires authentication.

declarations.name: SurfaceRouteOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Options for the surface explorer route.

properties.name: surface
properties.doc_comment: The generated app surface to serve.

declarations.doc_comment:
Create an authenticated route spec that serves the `AppSurface` as JSON.

Surface data reveals API structure (routes, auth, schemas), so this
requires authentication like the server status route.

modules.module_comment:
Common route spec factories for fuz_app consumers.

Generic HTTP route factories with no auth-domain dependencies.
Auth-aware route factories (account status) live in `auth/account_routes.ts`.

declarations.name: TableInfo
declarations.doc_comment: Table metadata from `information_schema`.

declarations.name: TableWithCount
declarations.doc_comment: Table info with row count.

declarations.name: PrimaryKeyInfo
declarations.doc_comment: Primary key constraint info.

declarations.name: ColumnInfo
declarations.doc_comment: Column metadata from `information_schema`.

declarations.name: DB_TABLE_ROWS_DEFAULT_LIMIT
declarations.doc_comment: Default page size for `GET /tables/:name` rows.

declarations.name: DB_TABLE_ROWS_LIMIT_MAX
declarations.doc_comment: Maximum page size for `GET /tables/:name` rows.

declarations.name: DbRouteOptions
declarations.doc_comment: Per-factory configuration for db routes.

properties.name: extra_stats
properties.doc_comment: Optional callback to provide app-specific stats in the health response.

properties.name: log
properties.doc_comment: Optional logger for server-side diagnostics (e.g. FK violation details).

declarations.name: create_db_route_specs
declarations.doc_comment: Create the db API route specs.

API route specs for database administration.

Generic PostgreSQL table browser using `information_schema`.
Provides: list tables, view columns/rows (paginated), delete rows by PK, health check.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_REQUEST_BODY
declarations.doc_comment: Request body failed Zod validation.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_JSON_BODY
declarations.doc_comment: Request body is not valid JSON or not an object.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_ROUTE_PARAMS
declarations.doc_comment: URL path params failed Zod validation.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_QUERY_PARAMS
declarations.doc_comment: URL query params failed Zod validation.

declarations.name: ERROR_AUTHENTICATION_REQUIRED
declarations.doc_comment: No valid session or bearer token.

declarations.name: ERROR_INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS
declarations.doc_comment: Authenticated but missing required role.

declarations.name: ERROR_CREDENTIAL_TYPE_REQUIRED
declarations.doc_comment:
Route requires a credential type the request didn't arrive on.
Symmetric with `ERROR_INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS` + `required_roles`:
the body carries `required_credential_types: ReadonlyArray<string>`
— what the route demanded, not what arrived. Today the only
credential gate is keeper (`['daemon_token']`); future gates
(`agent_token`, `group_actor_token`) reuse the same literal and
label themselves through the array.

declarations.name: ERROR_RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED
declarations.doc_comment: Rate limiter rejected the request.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_CREDENTIALS
declarations.doc_comment: Username or password is wrong (intentionally vague for enumeration prevention).

declarations.name: ERROR_PAYLOAD_TOO_LARGE
declarations.doc_comment: Request body exceeds the maximum allowed size.

declarations.name: ERROR_FORBIDDEN_ORIGIN
declarations.doc_comment: Request origin not in allowlist.

declarations.name: ERROR_BEARER_REJECTED_BROWSER
declarations.doc_comment: Bearer token sent with Origin/Referer header (browser context).

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_TOKEN
declarations.doc_comment: Bearer token failed validation (missing, malformed, or revoked).

declarations.name: ERROR_ACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND
declarations.doc_comment: Token references a deleted account.

declarations.name: ERROR_ACTOR_REQUIRED
declarations.doc_comment:
Multi-actor account requires the request to carry an explicit `acting`
field naming the actor the request is acting as, so the dispatcher's
authorization phase doesn't pick a default actor silently. Returned
with the available actors so the client can prompt.

declarations.name: ERROR_ACTOR_NOT_ON_ACCOUNT
declarations.doc_comment:
Supplied `acting` field does not name an actor on the authenticated
account.

declarations.name: ERROR_NO_ACTORS_ON_ACCOUNT
declarations.doc_comment:
Authenticated account exists but has no actors. Server invariant
violation — signup / bootstrap always create an actor in the same
transaction. Surfaced from the dispatcher's authorization phase as a
500 so the operator sees the corruption signal rather than a confusing
4xx. Distinct from `ERROR_ACCOUNT_VANISHED`: the actor list was
enumerated successfully and came back empty.

declarations.name: ERROR_ACCOUNT_VANISHED
declarations.doc_comment:
Authentication validated an account, but a follow-up read in the
authorization phase came back null — the account or its named actor
row was deleted between the credential check and the dispatcher's
`build_request_context` / `build_account_context` step. Torn read,
not a missing-actor invariant violation. Surfaced as 500 so the
operator sees the race signal; clients can retry. Distinct from
`ERROR_ACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND` (stale token referencing a long-deleted
account, raised at credential validation) and
`ERROR_NO_ACTORS_ON_ACCOUNT` (the actor list enumerated empty).

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_DAEMON_TOKEN
declarations.doc_comment: Daemon token header present but malformed or not matching current/previous token.

declarations.name: ERROR_KEEPER_ACCOUNT_NOT_CONFIGURED
declarations.doc_comment: Daemon token valid but keeper account not yet resolved (pre-bootstrap).

declarations.name: ERROR_KEEPER_ACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND
declarations.doc_comment: Keeper account ID set but account row not found.

declarations.name: ERROR_ALREADY_BOOTSTRAPPED
declarations.doc_comment: Bootstrap lock already acquired — system already bootstrapped.

declarations.name: ERROR_TOKEN_FILE_MISSING
declarations.doc_comment: Bootstrap token file not found on disk.

declarations.name: ERROR_NO_MATCHING_INVITE
declarations.doc_comment: No unclaimed invite matches the signup credentials.

declarations.name: ERROR_SIGNUP_CONFLICT
declarations.doc_comment: Signup conflict — username or email already taken (intentionally vague for enumeration prevention).

declarations.name: ERROR_INVITE_NOT_FOUND
declarations.doc_comment: Invite not found (for delete operations).

declarations.name: ERROR_INVITE_DUPLICATE
declarations.doc_comment: An unclaimed invite already exists for this email or username.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVITE_ACCOUNT_EXISTS_USERNAME
declarations.doc_comment: An account already exists with this invite's username.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVITE_ACCOUNT_EXISTS_EMAIL
declarations.doc_comment: An account already exists with this invite's email.

declarations.name: ERROR_ROLE_NOT_WEB_GRANTABLE
declarations.doc_comment: Admin tried to grant a role that is not web-grantable.

declarations.name: ERROR_ROLE_GRANT_NOT_FOUND
declarations.doc_comment: Role grant ID not found or not owned by the target actor.

declarations.name: ERROR_INVALID_EVENT_TYPE
declarations.doc_comment: Query parameter `event_type` is not a valid audit event type.

declarations.name: ERROR_FOREIGN_KEY_VIOLATION
declarations.doc_comment: DELETE blocked by a foreign key constraint.

declarations.name: ERROR_TABLE_NOT_FOUND
declarations.doc_comment: Table name not found in `information_schema`.

declarations.name: ERROR_TABLE_NO_PRIMARY_KEY
declarations.doc_comment: Table has no primary key constraint (cannot delete by PK).

declarations.name: ERROR_ROW_NOT_FOUND
declarations.doc_comment: Row with the given PK value not found.

declarations.name: ERROR_DATABASE_CONNECTION_FAILED
declarations.doc_comment: Database health-check query failed (connectivity or query error).

declarations.name: ApiError
declarations.doc_comment: Base API error — all JSON error responses have at least `{error: string}`.

declarations.name: ValidationError
declarations.doc_comment:
Input validation error — returned when params / query / body fails Zod
parsing, or when the request body is not valid JSON.

`error` is one of the four validation codes the framework emits.
`issues` carries Zod's validation issues for diagnostic display on the
three schema-failure cases (`invalid_request_body`,
`invalid_route_params`, `invalid_query_params`). The `invalid_json_body`
case (request body parse failure or non-object root) emits no `issues`,
so the field is optional.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ error: ZodEnum<{ invalid_request_body: "invalid_request_body"; invalid_json_body: "invalid_json_body"; invalid_route_params: "invalid_route_params"; invalid_query_params: "invalid_query_params"; }>; issues: ZodOptional<...>; }, $loose>

declarations.name: PermissionError
declarations.doc_comment:
Permission error — returned by `require_role()` and the dispatcher's
post-authorization role gate when the actor's role_grants don't include any
of the route's `auth.roles`.

`required_roles` carries the full disjunction the route declared
(`auth.roles` from the new flat-record shape). Single-role specs surface
as a one-element array; multi-role disjunctions show every admittable
role so clients can render targeted copy ("requires admin or steward").

declarations.name: CredentialTypeRequiredError
declarations.doc_comment:
Credential-type error — returned by the dispatcher's post-authorization
credential gate (and the `require_credential_types` REST middleware) when
the request's credential type isn't in the route's
`auth.credential_types` allowlist.

`required_credential_types` carries what the route declared
(`['daemon_token']` for keeper; future gates carry their own labels).
Symmetric with `PermissionError`'s `required_roles`: clients see what
the route demanded, not what their credential is.

declarations.name: RateLimitError
declarations.doc_comment: Rate limit error — returned when a rate limiter rejects the request.

declarations.name: PayloadTooLargeError
declarations.doc_comment: Payload too large error — returned when the request body exceeds the size limit.

declarations.name: ForeignKeyError
declarations.doc_comment: Foreign key violation error — returned when a delete is blocked by references.

declarations.name: ActorRequiredError
declarations.doc_comment:
Authorization-phase failure shapes. Surfaced when the dispatcher's
`apply_authorization_phase` rejects a request before the handler runs —
the route is acting-aware (input declares `acting?: ActingActor` or
auth requires role_grants), but actor resolution failed.

400: `actor_required` (with `available[]`) for unspecified-actor on
a multi-actor account; `actor_not_on_account` for a supplied actor
id that doesn't belong to the authenticated account.

500: `no_actors_on_account` for a signup-invariant violation (the
actor list enumerated empty); `account_vanished` for a torn-read
race (account/actor row deleted between credential validation and
the dispatcher's follow-up read).

Used by `derive_error_schemas` when `auth.actor !== 'none'` so the
merged error surface matches what the dispatcher actually emits.
declarations.type_signature: ZodObject<{ error: ZodLiteral<"actor_required">; available: ZodArray<ZodObject<{ id: ZodString; name: ZodString; }, $loose>>; }, $loose>

declarations.name: RouteErrorSchemas
declarations.doc_comment:
Error schema map — maps HTTP status codes to Zod schemas.

Used on `RouteSpec.errors` and internally by `derive_error_schemas`.

declarations.name: RateLimitKey
declarations.doc_comment:
Rate limit key type — declares what a route or RPC action's rate limiter
is keyed on.

- `'ip'` — per-IP rate limiting (bootstrap, password change, bearer auth)
- `'account'` — per-account rate limiting. On REST auth routes the key is
  the submitted identifier (login). On RPC actions (post-auth) the key is
  the resolved actor id (`request_context.actor.id`) — separate namespace.
- `'both'` — both keys.

declarations.name: DeriveErrorSchemasOptions
declarations.doc_comment:
Derive error schemas from a route's auth requirement, input schema, and rate limit config.

Returns the error schemas that middleware will auto-produce for this route.
Route handlers can declare additional error schemas via `RouteSpec.errors`;
explicit entries override auto-derived ones for the same status code.

Derivation rules under the new flat-record auth shape:
- **Has input / params / query schema**: 400 (`ValidationError`).
- **`auth.account === 'required'`** or **`auth.actor === 'required'`**: 401
  (`ApiError`) — pre-validation 401 fires when the credential isn't there.
  `'optional'` does not derive 401.
- **`auth.roles?.length`**: 403 (`PermissionError` carrying `required_roles`).
- **`auth.credential_types?.length`**: 403 (`CredentialTypeRequiredError`
  carrying `required_credential_types` — symmetric with `PermissionError`).
  Today the only credential gate is keeper; future gates reuse the literal.
- **`auth.actor !== 'none'`** (`'optional'` or `'required'`): extends 400
  with `ActorRequiredError` / `ActorNotOnAccountError` and adds 500 union
  of `NoActorsOnAccountError` / `AccountVanishedError`. The dispatcher's
  authorization phase emits these whenever it tries to resolve an actor.
- **rate_limit**: 429 (`RateLimitError` with `retry_after`).

declarations.name: derive_error_schemas
declarations.type_signature: ({ auth, has_input, has_params, has_query, rate_limit, }: DeriveErrorSchemasOptions): Partial<Record<number, ZodType<unknown, unknown, $ZodTypeInternals<unknown, unknown>>>>

modules.module_comment:
Standard error response schemas and error code constants for fuz_app routes.

Defines `ERROR_*` constants (single source of truth for machine-parseable
error codes), Zod schemas for error response shapes, a type for error schema
maps, and `derive_error_schemas` to auto-populate middleware-produced errors
from a route's auth requirement and input schema.

Used in `RouteSpec.errors` and `MiddlewareSpec.errors` for surface
introspection and DEV-mode validation.

declarations.name: IP_LITERAL_CHARS
declarations.doc_comment:
Allowed character set for a bare IP literal.

Covers the union of IPv4 (digits + `.`), IPv6 (hex digits + `:`), and
IPv4-mapped IPv6 forms (`::ffff:127.0.0.1`). Anything outside this
set — brackets, whitespace, control bytes, letters g–z — disqualifies
the input from parsing.

Same regex `proxy.ts`'s `validate_ip_strict` uses; exported here so
both modules can share one source of truth.

declarations.name: canonicalize_ip
declarations.doc_comment:
Canonicalize an IP address string.

Returns the RFC 5952 canonical form for parseable IPv4 or IPv6
input. Returns the input unchanged (only lowercased) when the input
is non-IP (`'unknown'`), malformed (`'attacker:controlled'`,
`'::1\n'`), or any string the strict char-set filter rejects.

**Idempotent.** `canonicalize_ip(canonicalize_ip(x)) === canonicalize_ip(x)`
for every input.

**Order-safe for IPv4-mapped IPv6.** The `::ffff:` prefix strip
runs AFTER the canonical emit because the canonical form of an
IPv4-mapped IPv6 address is the dotted form (`::ffff:127.0.0.1`,
not `::ffff:7f00:1`). Stripping before canonicalize would miss the
full-hex form. Closes the
`normalize_ipv4_mapped_collapse_is_order_safe` test from the Rust
port.
examples:
canonicalize_ip('::0001')                    // → '::1'
canonicalize_ip('0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1')           // → '::1'
canonicalize_ip('2001:0DB8::0001')           // → '2001:db8::1'
canonicalize_ip('::ffff:127.0.0.1')          // → '127.0.0.1'
canonicalize_ip('0:0:0:0:0:ffff:7f00:1')     // → '127.0.0.1'
canonicalize_ip('::ffff:1')                  // → '::ffff:1' (NOT IPv4-mapped — group[5] is 0, not ffff)
canonicalize_ip('127.0.0.1')                 // → '127.0.0.1'
canonicalize_ip('not-an-ip')                 // → 'not-an-ip' (passes through)
canonicalize_ip('::1\n')                     // → '::1\n' (fails char-set; passes through)
canonicalize_ip('203.0.113.1:8080')          // → '203.0.113.1:8080' (passes through; validate_ip_strict rejects)

declarations.name: ipv6_bigint_to_canonical
declarations.doc_comment:
Convert a 128-bit IPv6 binary value into its RFC 5952 canonical string form.

- IPv4-mapped (groups[0..5] = 0, groups[5] = 0xffff) emits the
  `::ffff:a.b.c.d` dotted form per RFC 5952 §5.
- Otherwise: lowercase hex with no leading zeros per group (§4.1),
  the longest run of consecutive zero groups (≥ 2 groups) is
  replaced with `::` (§4.2.1, §4.2.3), and on equal-length runs the
  first one wins (§4.2.3). Single-zero groups stay as `0` (§4.2.2).

Pure helper exported for the test suite to exercise the
canonicalization invariants directly without a full
`convertIPv6ToBinary` round-trip.

throws.description: `bits` is negative or exceeds 128 bits

parameters.name: bits

modules.module_comment:
IP address canonicalization — collapse equivalent string forms into a
single key per RFC 5952 (IPv6) plus the dotted form for IPv4-mapped
IPv6 addresses.

**Why this exists.** Without canonicalization, the four representations
`::1`, `::01`, `::0001`, and `0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1` are the same IPv6 address
but produce four distinct strings — so an attacker rotating
equivalent forms behind a trusted-passthrough proxy could defeat
per-IP rate limiting (each form gets a fresh bucket) and pollute
`audit_log.ip` forensics. The collision can extend to IPv4-mapped
IPv6 forms (`::ffff:127.0.0.1` vs `0:0:0:0:0:ffff:7f00:1` vs the
bare `127.0.0.1`) — three keys for one address.

Canonicalization runs through {@link canonicalize_ip} which:

1. Lowercases and char-set filters (`IP_LITERAL_CHARS`) — non-IP
   strings (`'unknown'`, `'attacker:controlled'`, `'::1\n'`) pass
   through unchanged so downstream strict validators can still
   reject them.
2. Parses via Hono's `convertIPv*ToBinary` family.
3. Re-emits the canonical RFC 5952 string (lowercase hex,
   longest-zero-run compressed, IPv4-mapped emitted in the dotted
   form mandated by RFC 5952 §5).
4. Strips the `::ffff:` prefix from dotted IPv4-mapped forms so the
   bucket collapses to plain IPv4 — the strip moves AFTER
   canonicalization because the dotted form is the only form the
   strip can recognize symmetrically.

Mirrors `zzz_server::proxy::normalize_ip` (landed 2026-05-16) which
uses the same parse-then-canonicalize-then-strip ordering for the
same rate-limit-key-poisoning surface.

declarations.name: UNKNOWN_ERROR_MESSAGE
declarations.doc_comment: Default message for unknown errors.

declarations.name: JsonrpcErrorName
declarations.doc_comment: Names of standard and general application JSON-RPC error codes.

declarations.name: JSONRPC_ERROR_CODES
declarations.doc_comment:
Standard JSON-RPC error codes (5) plus general application codes (10).

Extensible — consumers add domain-specific codes to their own objects
by casting `as JsonrpcErrorCode`. Application codes use the -32000 to
-32099 range reserved by the JSON-RPC spec.

Frozen with `Object.freeze` to convert accidental mutation (test
cross-contamination, cast escapes) into loud TypeErrors. Spread into
a fresh object to extend.

declarations.name: jsonrpc_error_messages
declarations.doc_comment:
Named constructors for `JsonrpcErrorObject` values.

Each function creates a JSON-RPC error object with the correct
code and a sensible default message. Used by the catch layer in
`apply_route_specs` to build response bodies.

Frozen so tests must compose new objects rather than monkey-patch.
declarations.type_signature: Readonly<Record<JsonrpcErrorName, (...args: any[]) => { [x: string]: unknown; code: -32700 | -32600 | -32601 | -32602 | -32603 | (number & $brand<"JsonrpcServerErrorCode">); message: string; data?: unknown; }>>

declarations.name: ThrownJsonrpcError
declarations.doc_comment:
Error class carrying a JSON-RPC error code — thrown by handlers,
caught by `apply_route_specs` and mapped to HTTP status + JSON-RPC error response.

Named for what it is: an error with a JSON-RPC error code that gets thrown.

members.name: constructor
members.type_signature: (code: -32700 | -32600 | -32601 | -32602 | -32603 | (number & $brand<"JsonrpcServerErrorCode">), message: string, data?: unknown, options?: ErrorOptions | undefined): ThrownJsonrpcError

declarations.name: jsonrpc_errors
declarations.doc_comment:
Named constructors for `ThrownJsonrpcError`.

Usage: `throw jsonrpc_errors.not_found('user')` or `throw jsonrpc_errors.forbidden()`.
declarations.type_signature: { readonly parse_error: (...args: any[]) => ThrownJsonrpcError; readonly invalid_request: (...args: any[]) => ThrownJsonrpcError; readonly method_not_found: (...args: any[]) => ThrownJsonrpcError; ... 11 more ...; readonly request_cancelled: (...args: any[]) => ThrownJsonrpcError; }

declarations.name: JSONRPC_ERROR_CODE_TO_HTTP_STATUS
declarations.doc_comment:
Maps JSON-RPC error codes to HTTP status codes.

Extensible — consumers with domain-specific error codes assign directly
(`JSONRPC_ERROR_CODE_TO_HTTP_STATUS[-32020] = 502`) at module load. The
lookup function reads at call time, so mutation is the supported
extension mechanism.

declarations.name: HTTP_STATUS_TO_JSONRPC_ERROR_CODE
declarations.doc_comment:
Maps HTTP status codes to JSON-RPC error codes (reverse mapping).

When multiple error codes map to the same HTTP status (e.g. parse_error
and invalid_request both map to 400), the last one wins. Use for
best-effort HTTP → JSON-RPC translation.

declarations.name: jsonrpc_error_code_to_http_status
declarations.doc_comment:
Map a JSON-RPC error code to an HTTP status code.

Returns 500 for unrecognized codes (consumer-defined codes
without a mapping default to internal server error). The return
is narrowed to Hono's `ContentfulStatusCode` so call sites can
pass the result to `c.json(body, status)` without `as any` —
499 (nginx "client closed request") is non-standard and gets
absorbed by the cast here rather than at every dispatcher branch.

declarations.name: http_status_to_jsonrpc_error_code
declarations.doc_comment:
Map an HTTP status code to a JSON-RPC error code.

Returns `internal_error` (-32603) for unrecognized status codes.

declarations.name: JSONRPC_ERROR_CODE_TO_NAME
declarations.doc_comment:
Reverse map of `JSONRPC_ERROR_CODES` — JSON-RPC error code → name.

Used by REST emitters that need a stable string identifier for the
code in their flat-shape error body (`{error: '<name>', ...}`)
without inventing a separate vocabulary. Built once at module load
from the canonical `JSONRPC_ERROR_CODES` map so the two cannot drift.

Consumer-defined codes outside the standard taxonomy are not present;
`jsonrpc_error_code_to_name` falls back to `'internal_error'` so the
REST shape always carries some reason rather than `undefined`.

declarations.name: jsonrpc_error_code_to_name
declarations.doc_comment:
Map a JSON-RPC error code to its canonical name (`'not_found'`,
`'forbidden'`, etc.). Falls back to `'internal_error'` for codes
outside the standard taxonomy so REST emitters that read this for
their `error` field always have a stable string to emit.

modules.module_comment:
JSON-RPC error infrastructure for fuz_app routes.

Provides error types, named constructors, and HTTP status mapping
for the throw/catch error pattern used by `apply_route_specs`.
Core error codes (5 standard + 10 general application). Domain-specific
codes stay in consumers — add by casting `as JsonrpcErrorCode`.

`JsonrpcErrorCode` and `JsonrpcErrorObject` types are Zod-inferred
from `http/jsonrpc.ts` — this module re-uses those as the single source
of truth.

Complementary to `http/error_schemas.ts`: that module is declarative
(Zod schemas for surface introspection), this one is runtime
(throw + catch + map).

declarations.name: create_jsonrpc_request
declarations.doc_comment: Creates a JSON-RPC request message.
declarations.type_signature: (method: string, params: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined, id: string | number): { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number; method: string; params?: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined; }
declarations.return_type: { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number; method: string; params?: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined; }

declarations.name: create_jsonrpc_notification
declarations.doc_comment: Creates a JSON-RPC notification message (no id, no response expected).
declarations.type_signature: (method: string, params: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined): { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; method: string; params?: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined; }
declarations.return_type: { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; method: string; params?: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined; }

declarations.name: create_jsonrpc_error_response
declarations.doc_comment: Creates a JSON-RPC error response message.
declarations.type_signature: (id: string | number | null, error: { [x: string]: unknown; code: -32700 | -32600 | -32601 | -32602 | -32603 | (number & $brand<"JsonrpcServerErrorCode">); message: string; data?: unknown; }): { ...; }
declarations.return_type: { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number | null; error: { [x: string]: unknown; code: -32700 | -32600 | -32601 | -32602 | -32603 | (number & $brand<"JsonrpcServerErrorCode">); message: string; data?: unknown; }; }

declarations.name: create_jsonrpc_error_response_from_thrown
declarations.doc_comment:
Creates a JSON-RPC error response from any error.
Handles `ThrownJsonrpcError` (preserves code/message/data) and
regular `Error` objects (maps to internal_error, includes stack in DEV).
declarations.type_signature: (id: string | number | null, error: unknown): { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number | null; error: { [x: string]: unknown; code: -32700 | -32600 | -32601 | -32602 | -32603 | (number & $brand<...>); message: string; data?: unknown; }; }

declarations.name: is_jsonrpc_request_id
declarations.doc_comment: Checks if a value is a valid JSON-RPC request id (string or finite number).

declarations.name: is_jsonrpc_object
declarations.doc_comment: Checks if a value is a JSON-RPC object (has `jsonrpc: '2.0'`).

declarations.name: is_jsonrpc_message
declarations.doc_comment: Checks if a value is any valid JSON-RPC message or batch array.
declarations.type_signature: (message: unknown): message is { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number; method: string; params?: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined; } | { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number; result: JSONType; } | { ...; } | { ...; } | ({ ...; } | ... 2 more ... | { ...; })[]

declarations.doc_comment: Checks if a value is a JSON-RPC request (has method + id).
declarations.type_signature: (message: unknown): message is { [x: string]: unknown; jsonrpc: "2.0"; id: string | number; method: string; params?: { [x: string]: unknown; } | undefined; }

declarations.doc_comment: Checks if a value is a JSON-RPC notification (has method, no id).

declarations.doc_comment: Checks if a value is a JSON-RPC success response (has result + id).
